subject: Math
description: Use tables, bar graphs, timelines and Venn diagrams to display data sets. The data may include fractions or decimals. Understand that spreadsheet tables and graphs can be used to display data.
description_plain: Your child can use tables, bar graphs, timelines, and Venn diagrams to show data, including fractions and decimals, and knows that spreadsheets can help with this.

subject: Math
description: Collect, organize, display and interpret data, including data collected over a period of time and data represented by fractions and decimals.
description_plain: Your child can collect, organize, show, and understand data, including data with fractions and decimals and data collected over time.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that translations, reflections and rotations preserve congruency and use them to show that two figures are congruent.
description_plain: Your child knows that moving, flipping, or turning shapes keeps them the same size and shape and can use this to show two shapes are the same.

subject: Math
description: Apply rotations (turns) of 90° clockwise or counterclockwise.
description_plain: Your child can turn shapes 90 degrees to the right or left.

subject: Math
description: Apply reflections (flips) to figures by reflecting over vertical or horizontal lines and relate reflections to lines of symmetry.
description_plain: Your child can flip shapes over vertical or horizontal lines and understand how this relates to symmetry.

subject: Math
description: Apply translations (slides) to figures.
description_plain: Your child can slide shapes to new places without changing their size or shape.

subject: Math
description: Use translations, reflections and rotations to establish congruency and understand symmetries.
description_plain: Your child can use slides, flips, and turns to show shapes are the same and understand symmetry.

subject: Math
description: Find the areas of geometric figures and real-world objects that can be divided into rectangular shapes. Use square units to label area measurements.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of shapes and objects by breaking them into rectangles and measuring in square units.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the area of a two-dimensional figure can be found by counting the total number of same size square units that cover a shape without gaps or overlaps. Justify why length and width are multiplied to find the area of a rectangle by breaking the rectangle into one unit by one unit squares and viewing these as grouped into rows and columns.
description_plain: Your child understands that area is found by counting square units that cover a shape and can explain why multiplying length by width works for rectangles.

subject: Math
description: Compare angles according to size. Classify angles as acute, right and obtuse.
description_plain: Your child can compare angles by size and sort them into acute, right, or obtuse categories.

subject: Math
description: Measure angles in geometric figures and real-world objects with a protractor or angle ruler.
description_plain: Your child can measure angles in shapes and real objects using a protractor or angle ruler.

subject: Math
description: Understand angle and area as measurable attributes of real-world and mathematical objects. Use various tools to measure angles and areas.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles and areas can be measured and uses tools to measure them in real and math objects.

subject: Math
description: Describe, classify and draw quadrilaterals, including squares, rectangles, trapezoids, rhombuses, parallelograms and kites. Recognize quadrilaterals in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child can describe, sort, and draw different four-sided shapes like squares, rectangles, trapezoids, rhombuses, parallelograms, and kites, and recognize them around them.

subject: Math
description: Describe, classify and sketch triangles, including equilateral, right, obtuse and acute triangles. Recognize triangles in various contexts
description_plain: Your child can describe, sort, and draw different types of triangles and spot triangles in everyday things.

subject: Math
description: Name, describe, classify and sketch polygons.
description_plain: Your child can name, describe, sort, and draw many-sided shapes called polygons.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplication, division and unknowns to represent a given problem situation using a number sentence. Use number sense, properties of multiplication, and the relationship between multiplication and division to find values for the unknowns that make the number sentences true.
description_plain: Your child can write number sentences with multiplication, division, and unknowns to show a problem and find the missing numbers using number sense and math properties.

subject: Math
description: Understand how to interpret number sentences involving multiplication, division and unknowns. Use real-world situations involving multiplication or division to represent number sentences.
description_plain: Your child understands number sentences with multiplication, division, and unknowns and can use real-life examples to write these sentences.

subject: Math
description: Use number sentences involving multiplication, division and unknowns to represent and solve real-world and mathematical problems; create real-world situations corresponding to number sentences.
description_plain: Your child can use number sentences with multiplication, division, and unknowns to solve problems and make up real-life situations that match those sentences.

subject: Math
description: Create and use input-output rules involving addition, subtraction, multiplication and division to solve problems in various contexts. Record the inputs and outputs in a chart or table.
description_plain: Your child can create rules using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division to solve problems and keep track of inputs and outputs in charts or tables.

subject: Math
description: Use input-output rules, tables and charts to represent patterns and relationships and to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child uses rules, tables, and charts to find patterns and solve problems in real life and math.

subject: Math
description: Round decimals to the nearest tenth.
description_plain: Your child can round decimal numbers to the nearest tenth.

subject: Math
description: Read and write tenths and hundredths in decimal and fraction notations using words and symbols; know the fraction and decimal equivalents for halves and fourths.
description_plain: Your child can read and write tenths and hundredths as decimals and fractions, and knows which fractions equal halves and fourths.

subject: Math
description: Compare and order decimals and whole numbers using place value, a number line and models such as grids and base 10 blocks.
description_plain: Your child can compare and put decimals and whole numbers in order using place value, number lines, and models like grids or blocks.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals with words and symbols; use place value to describe decimals in terms of thousands, hundreds, tens, ones, tenths, hundredths and thousandths.
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimals with words and symbols and explain decimals using place values like tenths, hundredths, and thousandths.

subject: Math
description: Use fraction models to add and subtract fractions with like denominators in real-world and mathematical situations. Develop a rule for addition and subtraction of fractions with like denominators.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions with the same bottom number using fraction models and can explain the rule for doing this.

subject: Math
description: Locate fractions on a number line. Use models to order and compare whole numbers and fractions, including mixed numbers and improper fractions.
description_plain: Your child can find fractions on a number line and use models to compare and order whole numbers and fractions, including mixed and improper fractions.

subject: Math
description: Represent equivalent fractions using fraction models such as parts of a set, fraction circles, fraction strips, number lines and other manipulatives. Use the models to determine equivalent fractions.
description_plain: Your child can show and find equal fractions using fraction models like sets, circles, strips, and number lines.

subject: Math
description: Represent and compare fractions and decimals in real-world and mathematical situations; use place value to understand how decimals represent quantities.
description_plain: Your child can work with fractions and decimals in real life and math, using place value to understand what decimals mean.

subject: Math
description: Use strategies and algorithms based on knowledge of place value, equality and properties of operations to divide multi-digit whole numbers by one- or two-digit numbers. Strategies may include mental strategies, partial quotients, the commutative, associative, and distributive properties and repeated subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can divide large whole numbers by one- or two-digit numbers using strategies like mental math, breaking numbers apart, and properties of operations.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step real-world and mathematical problems requiring the use of addition, subtraction and multiplication of multi-digit whole numbers. Use various strategies, including the relationship between operations, the use of technology, and the context of the problem to assess the reasonableness of results.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems using addition, subtraction, and multiplication of large numbers and check if answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Estimate products and quotients of multi-digit whole numbers by using rounding, benchmarks and place value to assess the reasonableness of results.
description_plain: Your child can estimate multiplication and division answers by rounding and using place value to see if answers are reasonable.

subject: Math
description: Multiply multi-digit numbers, using efficient and generalizable procedures, based on knowledge of place value, including standard algorithms.
description_plain: Your child can multiply large numbers using efficient methods based on place value, including standard algorithms.

subject: Math
description: Use an understanding of place value to multiply a number by 10, 100 and 1000.
description_plain: Your child understands how to multiply numbers by 10, 100, and 1000 using place value.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with multiplication and division facts.
description_plain: Your child knows multiplication and division facts quickly and accurately.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate mastery of multiplication and division basic facts; multiply multi-digit numbers; solve real-world and mathematical problems using arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child masters basic multiplication and division facts, multiplies large numbers, and solves real-life and math problems using these skills.

subject: Math
description: Identify pennies, nickels and dimes; find the value of a group of these coins, up to one dollar.
description_plain: Your child can identify pennies, nickels, and dimes and find the total value of a group of these coins up to one dollar.

subject: Math
description: Tell time to the hour and half-hour.
description_plain: Your child can tell time to the hour and half-hour.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object in terms of multiple copies of another object.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long an object is by using multiple copies of another object.

subject: Math
description: Use basic concepts of measurement in real-world and mathematical situations involving length, time and money.
description_plain: Your child uses basic measuring ideas to understand length, time, and money in real life and math.

subject: Math
description: Compose (combine) and decompose (take apart) two- and three-dimensional figures such as triangles, squares, rectangles, circles, rectangular prisms and cylinders.
description_plain: Your child can put together and take apart 2D and 3D shapes like triangles, squares, rectangles, circles, rectangular prisms, and cylinders.

subject: Math
description: Describe characteristics of two- and three-dimensional objects, such as triangles, squares, rectangles, circles, rectangular prisms, cylinders, cones and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can describe features of 2D and 3D shapes like triangles, squares, rectangles, circles, rectangular prisms, cylinders, cones, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Describe characteristics of basic shapes. Use basic shapes to compose and decompose other objects in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child can describe basic shapes and use them to make and break apart other objects.

subject: Math
description: Use addition or subtraction basic facts to represent a given problem situation using a number sentence.
description_plain: Your child can write number sentences using addition or subtraction facts to show a problem.

subject: Math
description:
Use number sense and models of addition and subtraction, such as objects and number lines, to identify the missing number in an equation such as:
  • 2 + 4 =
  • 3 + = 7
  • 5 = – 3.
description_plain: Your child uses objects and number lines to find missing numbers in addition and subtraction equations.

subject: Math
description: Determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true.
description_plain: Your child can tell if addition and subtraction equations are true or false.

subject: Math
description: Represent real-world situations involving addition and subtraction basic facts, using objects and number sentences.
description_plain: Your child can use objects and number sentences to show real-life situations involving addition and subtraction facts.

subject: Math
description: Use number sentences involving addition and subtraction basic facts to represent and solve real-world and mathematical problems; create real-world situations corresponding to number sentences.
description_plain: Your child uses addition and subtraction number sentences to solve problems and make up real-life situations that match those sentences.

subject: Math
description: Create simple patterns using objects, pictures, numbers and rules. Identify possible rules to complete or extend patterns. Patterns may be repeating, growing or shrinking. Calculators can be used to create and explore patterns.
description_plain: Your child can make simple repeating or growing patterns using objects, pictures, or numbers and figure out the rules to continue them.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and create patterns; use rules to describe patterns.
description_plain: Your child will recognize patterns and create their own, using rules to explain how the patterns work.

subject: Math
description: Recognize the relationship between counting and addition and subtraction. Skip count by 2s, 5s, and 10s.
description_plain: Your child understands how counting relates to adding and subtracting and can count by 2s, 5s, and 10s.

subject: Math
description: Compose and decompose numbers up to 12 with an emphasis on making ten.
description_plain: Your child can break apart and put together numbers up to 12, focusing on making groups of ten.

subject: Math
description: Use words, pictures, objects, length-based models (connecting cubes), numerals and number lines to model and solve addition and subtraction problems in part-part-total, adding to, taking away from and comparing situations.
description_plain: Your child can use words, pictures, objects, and number lines to solve addition and subtraction problems by joining, taking away, or comparing amounts.

subject: Math
description: Use a variety of models and strategies to solve addition and subtraction problems in real-world and mathematical contexts.
description_plain: Your child will use different tools and methods to solve addition and subtraction problems in everyday situations and math exercises.

subject: Math
description: Use counting and comparison skills to create and analyze bar graphs and tally charts.
description_plain: Your child can count and compare information to make and understand bar graphs and tally charts.

subject: Math
description: Use words to describe the relative size of numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use words to explain if numbers are bigger, smaller, or about the same.

subject: Math
description: Compare and order whole numbers up to 120.
description_plain: Your child can compare and put whole numbers up to 120 in order from smallest to largest or vice versa.

subject: Math
description: Find a number that is 10 more or 10 less than a given number.
description_plain: Your child can find a number that is 10 more or 10 less than a given number.

subject: Math
description: Count, with and without objects, forward and backward from any given number up to 120.
description_plain: Your child can count forwards and backwards from any number up to 120, with or without objects.

subject: Math
description: Read, write and represent whole numbers up to 120. Representations may include numerals, addition and subtraction, pictures, tally marks, number lines and manipulatives, such as bundles of sticks and base 10 blocks.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and show whole numbers up to 120 using numbers, pictures, tally marks, number lines, or objects.

subject: Math
description: Use place value to describe whole numbers between 10 and 100 in terms of tens and ones.
description_plain: Your child understands numbers between 10 and 100 by breaking them into tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: Count, compare and represent whole numbers up to 120, with an emphasis on groups of tens and ones.
description_plain: Your child can count, compare, and show whole numbers up to 120, focusing on groups of tens and ones.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast buildings and other technologies from earlier times and today.
description_plain: Your child can tell how buildings and technology from long ago are different from those we use today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast family life from earlier times and today.
description_plain: Your child can explain how family life today is different from family life in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how people lived at a particular time in the past, based on information found in historical records and artifacts.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people lived long ago by looking at old records and objects.

subject: Social Studies
description: Ask basic historical questions about a past event in one's family, school or local community.
description_plain: Your child can ask simple questions about past events in their family, school, or community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create a timeline that identifies at least three events from one's own life.
description_plain: Your child can make a timeline showing at least three important events from their own life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare physical and human characteristics of a local place and a place far away on a globe or map (such as a place in an equatorial or polar region).
description_plain: Your child can compare features of a nearby place and a faraway place using maps or globes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use relative location words and absolute location words to identify the location of a specific place; explain why or when it is important to use absolute versus relative location.
description_plain: Your child can use words like 'near' or 'north' and exact locations like coordinates to say where a place is and explain when to use each.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create sketch maps to illustrate spatial information about familiar places; describe spatial information found on maps.
description_plain: Your child can draw simple maps of familiar places and explain what the maps show.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that people trade (voluntarily) when they each expect to be better off after doing so.
description_plain: Your child understands that people trade with each other because they both expect to gain something.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define scarcity as not having enough of something to satisfy everyone's wants; give examples.
description_plain: Your child knows that scarcity means not having enough of something for everyone and can give examples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe some costs and benefits of alternative choices made by families.
description_plain: Your child can talk about some good and bad things about different choices families make.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify characteristics of effective rules; participate in a process to establish rules.
description_plain: Your child can name what makes a good rule and help make rules with others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the president of the United States; explain that the president is elected by the people.
description_plain: Your child can name the president of the United States and knows that people vote to choose the president.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why and when the Pledge of Allegiance is recited; provide examples of basic flag etiquette.
description_plain: Your child can explain why we say the Pledge of Allegiance and knows some basic rules about how to treat the flag.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate ways good citizens participate in the civic life of their community; explain why participation is important.
description_plain: Your child can show ways to be a good citizen in their community and explain why it matters.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how groups are reviving and maintaining their traditional cultures, identities and distinctiveness in the context of increasing globalization. (The New Global Era: 1989 to Present)
description_plain: Your child understands how groups keep their traditional cultures and identities even as the world becomes more connected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess the influence of television, the Internet and other media on cultural identity and social and political movements. (The New Global Era: 1989 to Present)
description_plain: Your child can explain how TV, the Internet, and other media affect culture and social or political movements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how movements and social conditions have affected the lives of women in different parts of the world. (The New Global Era: 1989 to Present)
description_plain: Your child can describe how social changes and movements have affected women's lives in different countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess the state of human rights around the world as described in the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights. (The New Global Era: 1989 to Present)
description_plain: Your child can talk about human rights around the world based on the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how Pacific Rim countries have achieved economic growth in recent decades. (The New Global Era: 1989 to Present)
description_plain: Your child can explain how countries around the Pacific Ocean have grown their economies in recent years.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe varieties of religious beliefs and practices in the contemporary world including Shamanism/Animism, Hinduism, Buddhism, Judaism, Christianity and Islam. (The New Global Era: 1989 to Present)
description_plain: Your child can describe different religions and their practices around the world today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the development of diasporic communities throughout the world due to regional conflicts, changing international labor demands and environmental factors. (The New Global Era: 1989 to Present)
description_plain: Your child can compare how groups of people have spread around the world because of conflicts, jobs, and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe causes of economic imbalances and social inequalities among the world's peoples in the post-colonial world and efforts made to close those gaps. (The New Global Era: 1989 to Present)
description_plain: Your child can explain why some countries and people have more money and power than others and what is being done to fix this.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe political challenges and struggles of newly independent countries during the Cold War era. (The World After World War II: 1950-1989)
description_plain: Your child can describe the political problems new countries faced during the Cold War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the major differences in the political and economic ideologies and values of the Western democracies and the Soviet bloc. (The World After World War II: 1950-1989)
description_plain: Your child can explain the main political and economic differences between Western democracies and the Soviet bloc.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze connections between revolutions, independence movements and social transformations during the Cold War era. (The World After World War II: 1950-1989)
description_plain: Your child can analyze how revolutions and independence movements caused social changes during the Cold War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Pose questions about a topic in world history; gather and organize a variety of primary and secondary sources related to the questions; analyze sources for credibility and bias; suggest possible answers and write a thesis statement; use sources to draw conclusions and support the thesis; and present supported findings and cite sources.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about world history, find and organize different sources, check if those sources are trustworthy, suggest answers, write a main idea, use sources to support it, and share their findings with proper credit.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of comparative advantage in the rise of the Indian market economy in the global economic system.
description_plain: Your child understands how having special skills or resources helped India’s economy grow and connect with the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of a market economy that exist in contemporary China; describe how China's changing economy has impacted the United States and the global economic system since 1970.
description_plain: Your child can identify features of China’s market economy and explain how China’s economic changes since 1970 have affected the U.S. and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the distribution and development of oil and water resources influence the economy and societies of Southwest Asia and North Africa.
description_plain: Your child understands how oil and water resources affect the economy and daily life in Southwest Asia and North Africa.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of Europe in the global economy today.
description_plain: Your child can describe Europe’s role in today’s global economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the changing role of Latin America in global trade networks.
description_plain: Your child understands how Latin America’s role in world trade has changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the impact of comparative advantage, the international division of labor, and de-industrialization on manufacturing regions and commercial districts within urban areas in the United States and Canada.
description_plain: Your child can explain how things like special skills, job changes, and factory closures have affected cities in the U.S. and Canada.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the changing patterns of industrialization and trade between the United States, and Canada or Mexico, have resulted in close connections between the countries in terms of manufacturing, energy and finance.
description_plain: Your child understands how changes in industry and trade have made the U.S., Canada, and Mexico work closely together in making goods, energy, and money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the impact of nationalist movements in the twentieth century on contemporary geopolitics in Southwest Asia and North Africa.
description_plain: Your child can describe how nationalist movements in the 1900s have shaped politics in Southwest Asia and North Africa today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe independence and nationalist movements in Sub-Saharan Africa and Asia, including India's independence movement.
description_plain: Your child understands independence and nationalist movements in Sub-Saharan Africa and Asia, including India’s fight for independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the physical and environmental features of Australia/Oceania affect human activity and settlement, including how the human populations have adapted to and changed the landscape differently over time.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Australia and Oceania’s land and environment affect where people live and how they have changed the land over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the physical and environmental features of Africa South of the Sahara affect human activity and settlement.
description_plain: Your child understands how Africa south of the Sahara’s land and environment influence where people live and what they do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the physical and environmental features of South Asia and Central Asia affect human activity and settlement.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the land and environment in South and Central Asia affect people’s lives and settlements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the physical and environmental features of East Asia and Southeast Asia affect human activity and settlement.
description_plain: Your child understands how East and Southeast Asia’s physical features affect where people live and how they live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the physical and environmental features of Southwest Asia and North Africa affect human activity and settlement.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the land and environment in Southwest Asia and North Africa affect human activities and settlements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the physical and environmental features of Europe and Russia affect human activity and settlement.
description_plain: Your child understands how Europe and Russia’s physical features influence where people live and how they settle.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the physical and environmental features of Latin America affect human activity and settlement.
description_plain: Your child can describe how Latin America’s land and environment affect where people live and their activities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the physical and environmental features of the United States and Canada affect human activity and settlement.
description_plain: Your child understands how the land and environment of the U.S. and Canada affect where people live and how they live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the locations of human populations and the cultural characteristics of Australia/Oceania.
description_plain: Your child can describe where people live in Australia and Oceania and what their cultures are like.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the locations of human populations and the cultural characteristics of Africa South of the Sahara, including the causes and effects of the demographic transition since 1945.
description_plain: Your child understands where people live in Africa south of the Sahara, their cultures, and how population changes since 1945 have affected the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the locations of human populations and the cultural characteristics of South Asia and Central Asia, including causes for the differences in population density in the region, and implications of population growth in South Asia on the future world population.
description_plain: Your child can describe where people live in South and Central Asia, why some areas have more people, and how population growth in South Asia affects the world’s future.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the locations of human populations and the cultural characteristics of East Asia and Southeast Asia, including how the demographic transition has influenced the region's population, economy and culture.
description_plain: Your child understands where people live in East and Southeast Asia, their cultures, and how population changes have influenced the region’s economy and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the locations of human populations and the cultural characteristics of Southwest Asia and North Africa.
description_plain: Your child can describe where people live in Southwest Asia and North Africa and what their cultures are like.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the locations of human populations and the cultural characteristics of Europe and Russia, including the role of migration patterns, and the impact of aging population and other effects of demographic transition
description_plain: Your child understands where people live in Europe and Russia, how migration and aging populations affect the region, and other population changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the locations of human populations and the cultural characteristics of Latin America, including how the contemporary pattern of cities resulted from a combination of pre-European contact, colonial, and industrial urban societies.
description_plain: Your child can describe where people live in Latin America, their cultures, and how cities developed from pre-European times through colonial and industrial periods.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the locations of human populations and the cultural characteristics of the United States and Canada.
description_plain: Your child understands where people live in the U.S. and Canada and what their cultures are like.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use appropriate geographic tools to analyze and explain the distribution of physical and human characteristics of places.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and other tools to study and explain where physical features and people are located.

subject: Social Studies
description: Formulate questions about topics in geography; pose possible answers; use geospatial technology to analyze problems and make decisions within a spatial context.
description_plain: Your child can ask geography questions, think of possible answers, and use technology like maps and GPS to study and solve problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and use various kinds of maps, including overlaying thematic maps, of places in the world; incorporate the "TODALSS" map basics, as well as points, lines and colored areas to display spatial information.
description_plain: Your child can make and use different types of maps, including layering information, and add important map details like titles and legends.

subject: Social Studies
description: Obtain and analyze geographic information from a variety of print and electronic sources to investigate places or answer specific geographic questions; provide rationale for its use.
description_plain: Your child can find and study geographic information from books and online sources to learn about places and answer questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why trade is mutually beneficial to countries; define and apply absolute and comparative advantage with respect to international trade.
description_plain: Your child understands why countries trade to help each other and can explain special advantages countries have in trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify characteristics of command, mixed, and market-based (capitalist) economies; classify the economic systems of countries in a given region.
description_plain: Your child can identify different types of economies like command, mixed, and market, and classify countries’ economies in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify factors which affect economic growth (percentage changes in Gross Domestic Product-GDP) and lead to a different standard of living in different countries.
description_plain: Your child understands what affects economic growth and why some countries have a higher standard of living than others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply reasoned decision-making techniques in making choices; explain why different governments faced with the same alternatives might make different choices.
description_plain: Your child can make thoughtful decisions and explain why different governments might choose different options.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how different types of governments reflect historically and culturally specific understandings of the relationships between the individual, government and society.
description_plain: Your child understands how different governments show unique ideas about the roles of individuals, government, and society based on history and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why governments belong to different types of economic alliances and international and regional organizations.
description_plain: Your child can explain why governments join different economic groups and international organizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Exhibit civic skills including participating in civic discussion on issues in the contemporary world, demonstrating respect for the opinions of people or groups who have different perspectives, and reaching consensus.
description_plain: Your child can join discussions about current world issues, respect others’ opinions, and work together to agree.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast traditions in a family with those of other families, including those from diverse backgrounds.
description_plain: Your child can compare their family traditions with those of other families, including families from different backgrounds.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe ways people learn about the past.
description_plain: Your child understands different ways people learn about the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of words to reference time in the past, present and future; identify the beginning, middle and end of historical stories.
description_plain: Your child can use words to talk about past, present, and future and identify the beginning, middle, and end of stories about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the physical and human characteristics of places, including real and imagined places.
description_plain: Your child can point out what makes places special by noticing things like land, buildings, and even places from stories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe a map and a globe as a representation of a space
description_plain: Your child understands that maps and globes are pictures that show where places are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe spatial information depicted in simple drawings and pictures.
description_plain: Your child can explain what simple drawings and pictures show about where things are located.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between goods (objects that can be seen or touched) and services (actions or activities).
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between things you can touch, like toys, and actions people do, like helping.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify goods and services that could satisfy a specific need or want.
description_plain: Your child can find things and actions that help meet a need or something they want.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between individual needs (conditions necessary to survive) and individual wants (conditions desired to be happy).
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between things they need to live and things they want to be happy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of rules in the school community and explain why they exist; describe incentives for following rules and consequences for breaking rules.
description_plain: Your child can name some school rules, explain why they are important, and understand rewards for following them and what happens if they don't.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe symbols, songs and traditions that identify our nation and state.
description_plain: Your child can talk about special symbols, songs, and traditions that show who we are as a country and state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate civic skills in a classroom that reflect an understanding of civic values.
description_plain: Your child shows good citizenship by practicing kindness, sharing, and helping in the classroom.

subject: Math
description: Assess the reasonableness of predictions using scatterplots by interpreting them in the original context.
description_plain: Your child can look at scatterplots and decide if predictions made from them make sense based on the situation.

subject: Math
description: Use a line of best fit to make statements about approximate rate of change and to make predictions about values not in the original data set.
description_plain: Your child uses a best-fit line on a scatterplot to understand how fast things change and to guess values not shown in the data.

subject: Math
description: Collect, display and interpret data using scatterplots. Use the shape of the scatterplot to informally estimate a line of best fit and determine an equation for the line. Use appropriate titles, labels and units. Know how to use graphing technology to display scatterplots and corresponding lines of best fit.
description_plain: Your child collects data, makes scatterplots with clear titles and labels, guesses a best-fit line, finds its equation, and uses tools like graphing calculators to help.

subject: Math
description: Interpret data using scatterplots and approximate lines of best fit. Use lines of best fit to draw conclusions about data.
description_plain: Your child reads scatterplots and best-fit lines to understand data and make conclusions.

subject: Math
description: Given a line on a coordinate system and the coordinates of a point not on the line, find lines through that point that are parallel and perpendicular to the given line, symbolically and graphically.
description_plain: Your child can find lines that go through a point and are either parallel or perpendicular to another line, both by drawing and using math.

subject: Math
description: Analyze polygons on a coordinate system by determining the slopes of their sides.
description_plain: Your child studies shapes on a graph by finding the slopes of their sides.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply the relationships between the slopes of parallel lines and between the slopes of perpendicular lines. Dynamic graphing software may be used to examine these relationships.
description_plain: Your child understands how slopes of parallel lines are the same and slopes of perpendicular lines are related, using tools to explore these ideas.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving parallel and perpendicular lines on a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child solves problems about parallel and perpendicular lines on graphs.

subject: Math
description: Informally justify the Pythagorean Theorem by using measurements, diagrams and computer software.
description_plain: Your child explains the Pythagorean Theorem by measuring, drawing, and using computer tools.

subject: Math
description: Determine the distance between two points on a horizontal or vertical line in a coordinate system. Use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between any two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child finds the distance between two points on a graph when they are in a straight line and uses the Pythagorean Theorem to find distances between any two points.

subject: Math
description: Use the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems involving right triangles.
description_plain: Your child uses the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems with right triangles.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving right triangles using the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.
description_plain: Your child solves problems about right triangles using the Pythagorean Theorem and its reverse.

subject: Math
description: Use the relationship between square roots and squares of a number to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child solves problems by understanding how squares and square roots are connected.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a system of linear equations may have no solution, one solution, or an infinite number of solutions. Relate the number of solutions to pairs of lines that are intersecting, parallel or identical. Check whether a pair of numbers satisfies a system of two linear equations in two unknowns by substituting the numbers into both equations.
description_plain: Your child knows that two lines can cross once, never cross, or be the same line, and checks if numbers solve two equations by plugging them in.

subject: Math
description: Represent relationships in various contexts using systems of linear equations. Solve systems of linear equations in two variables symbolically, graphically and numerically.
description_plain: Your child uses pairs of equations to show relationships and solves them by graphing, calculating, or using symbols.

subject: Math
description: Represent relationships in various contexts with equations and inequalities involving the absolute value of a linear expression. Solve such equations and inequalities and graph the solutions on a number line.
description_plain: Your child works with equations and inequalities that use absolute values, solves them, and shows answers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear inequalities using properties of inequalities. Graph the solutions on a number line.
description_plain: Your child solves inequalities step-by-step and shows the answers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Use linear inequalities to represent relationships in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child uses inequalities to show relationships in different situations.

subject: Math
description: Express linear equations in slope-intercept, point-slope and standard forms, and convert between these forms. Given sufficient information, find an equation of a line.
description_plain: Your child writes linear equations in different forms, changes between these forms, and finds an equation when given enough information.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step equations in one variable. Solve for one variable in a multi-variable equation in terms of the other variables. Justify the steps by identifying the properties of equalities used.
description_plain: Your child solves equations with one variable in multiple steps and rewrites equations to solve for one variable, explaining each step.

subject: Math
description: Use linear equations to represent situations involving a constant rate of change, including proportional and non-proportional relationships.
description_plain: Your child uses linear equations to show situations where things change at a steady rate, including when the change is proportional or not.

subject: Math
description: Represent real-world and mathematical situations using equations and inequalities involving linear expressions. Solve equations and inequalities symbolically and graphically. Interpret solutions in the original context.
description_plain: Your child represents real-life and math problems with linear equations and inequalities, solves them using symbols and graphs, and explains what the answers mean.

subject: Math
description: Justify steps in generating equivalent expressions by identifying the properties used, including the properties of algebra. Properties include the associative, commutative and distributive laws, and the order of operations, including grouping symbols.
description_plain: Your child explains each step when changing expressions by naming the math rules used, like how numbers can be grouped or moved.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate algebraic expressions, including expressions containing radicals and absolute values, at specified values of their variables.
description_plain: Your child finds the value of expressions, including those with roots and absolute values, by plugging in numbers for variables.

subject: Math
description: Generate equivalent numerical and algebraic expressions and use algebraic properties to evaluate expressions.
description_plain: Your child creates expressions that mean the same thing and uses math rules to work with them.

subject: Math
description: Represent geometric sequences using equations, tables, graphs and verbal descriptions, and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child shows geometric sequences with equations, tables, graphs, and words, and uses them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Represent arithmetic sequences using equations, tables, graphs and verbal descriptions, and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child shows arithmetic sequences using equations, tables, graphs, and words, and solves problems with them.

subject: Math
description: Identify how coefficient changes in the equation f(x) = mx + b affect the graphs of linear functions. Know how to use graphing technology to examine these effects.
description_plain: Your child notices how changing numbers in the equation f(x) = mx + b changes the graph and uses graphing tools to explore this.

subject: Math
description: Identify graphical properties of linear functions including slopes and intercepts. Know that the slope equals the rate of change, and that the y-intercept is zero when the function represents a proportional relationship.
description_plain: Your child understands parts of a graph like slope and intercepts, knowing slope shows how fast something changes and the y-intercept is zero for proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Represent linear functions with tables, verbal descriptions, symbols, equations and graphs; translate from one representation to another.
description_plain: Your child shows linear functions using tables, words, symbols, equations, and graphs, and can switch between these forms.

subject: Math
description: Recognize linear functions in real-world and mathematical situations; represent linear functions and other functions with tables, verbal descriptions, symbols and graphs; solve problems involving these functions and explain results in the original context.
description_plain: Your child recognizes linear functions in real life and math, represents them in different ways, solves related problems, and explains the answers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a geometric sequence is a non-linear function that can be expressed in the form f(x)=ab<sup>x</sup>, where x = 0, 1, 2, 3,…
description_plain: Your child understands that a geometric sequence is a pattern where each number is multiplied by the same amount, and it can be written as f(x) = a times b to the power of x.

subject: Math
description: Understand that an arithmetic sequence is a linear function that can be expressed in the form f(x) = mx + b , where x = 0, 1, 2, 3,…
description_plain: Your child understands that an arithmetic sequence is a pattern where the numbers increase or decrease by the same amount each time, and it can be written as f(x) = m times x plus b.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function is linear if it can be expressed in the form f(x) = mx + b or if its graph is a straight line.
description_plain: Your child understands that a function is linear if it can be written as f(x) = m times x plus b or if its graph is a straight line.

subject: Math
description: Use linear functions to represent relationships in which changing the input variable by some amount leads to a change in the output variable that is a constant times that amount.
description_plain: Your child can use linear functions to show situations where changing one value by a certain amount causes another value to change by a constant multiple of that amount.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function is a relationship between an independent variable and a dependent variable in which the value of the independent variable determines the value of the dependent variable. Use functional notation, such as f(x), to represent such relationships.
description_plain: Your child understands that a function links one value (input) to another value (output), and they can use symbols like f(x) to show this relationship.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of function in real-world and mathematical situations, and distinguish between linear and nonlinear functions.
description_plain: Your child understands what functions are in real life and math, and can tell the difference between linear (straight-line) and nonlinear functions.

subject: Math
description: Express approximations of very large and very small numbers using scientific notation; understand how calculators display numbers in scientific notation. Multiply and divide numbers expressed in scientific notation, express the answer in scientific notation, using the correct number of significant digits when physical measurements are involved.
description_plain: Your child can write very big or very small numbers using scientific notation, understand how calculators show these numbers, and multiply or divide them while keeping the right number of significant digits.

subject: Math
description: Know and apply the properties of positive and negative integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child knows the rules for positive and negative exponents and can use them to rewrite number expressions in different but equal ways.

subject: Math
description: Determine rational approximations for solutions to problems involving real numbers.
description_plain: Your child can find close, simple number answers (rational approximations) to problems involving real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare real numbers; locate real numbers on a number line. Identify the square root of a positive integer as an integer, or if it is not an integer, locate it as a real number between two consecutive positive integers.
description_plain: Your child can compare real numbers, place them on a number line, and find where square roots of positive numbers fit between whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Classify real numbers as rational or irrational. Know that when a square root of a positive integer is not an integer, then it is irrational. Know that the sum of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational, and the product of a non-zero rational number and an irrational number is irrational.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a real number is rational or irrational, knows that square roots of some numbers are irrational, and understands how adding or multiplying rational and irrational numbers works.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, compare, classify and represent real numbers, and use them to solve problems in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, compare, sort, and use real numbers to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major Minnesota political figures, ideas and industries that have shaped or continue to shape Minnesota and the United States today. (The United States in a New Global Age: 1980-present)
description_plain: Your child can name important Minnesota leaders, ideas, and industries that have shaped Minnesota and the U.S. from 1980 to today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the push-pull factors that bring the Hmong, East African, Hispanic, Asian Indian and other immigrants and refugees to Minnesota; compare and contrast their experiences with those of earlier Minnesota immigrant groups in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. (The United States in a New Global Age: 1980-present)
description_plain: Your child can explain why groups like the Hmong, East African, Hispanic, and Asian Indian people moved to Minnesota and compare their experiences to earlier immigrants from the 1800s and early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the response of Minnesotans to global conflicts and displaced peoples since 1945. (Post-World War II United States: 1945-1989)
description_plain: Your child can describe how Minnesotans responded to world conflicts and refugees since 1945.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe civil rights and conservation movements in Post-World War II Minnesota, including the role of Minnesota leaders. (Post-World War II United States: 1945-1989)
description_plain: Your child can describe civil rights and conservation movements in Minnesota after World War II and the role Minnesota leaders played.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of economic changes in Minnesota during the Cold War era; describe the impact of these changes on Minnesota's people. (Post-World War II United States: 1945-1989)
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how Minnesota’s economy changed during the Cold War and how these changes affected people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify contributions of Minnesota and its people to World War II; describe the impact of the war on the home front and Minnesota society after the war. (The Great Depression and World War II: 1920-1945)
description_plain: Your child can identify Minnesota’s contributions to World War II and explain how the war affected life in Minnesota during and after the war.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create a timeline of key events leading to World War II; describe how Minnesotans influenced, and were influenced by, the debates over United States involvement. (The Great Depression and World War II: 1920-1945)
description_plain: Your child can make a timeline of important events leading to World War II and explain how Minnesotans influenced and were affected by debates about joining the war.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe political and social impact of the Great Depression and New Deal in Minnesota, including the increased conflict between big business and organized labor. (The Great Depression and World War II: 1920-1945)
description_plain: Your child can describe how the Great Depression and New Deal affected Minnesota’s politics and society, including conflicts between big business and workers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the major cultural and social transformations of the 1920s changed the lifestyle of Minnesotans. (The Great Depression and World War II: 1920-1945)
description_plain: Your child can explain how cultural and social changes in the 1920s changed how Minnesotans lived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the political and social culture of Minnesota during World War I and how it affected Minnesotans. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child can describe Minnesota’s political and social life during World War I and how it affected people there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe Minnesota and federal American Indian policy of the late nineteenth and twentieth centuries and its impact on Anishinaabe and Dakota people, especially in the areas of education, land ownership and citizenship. (Development of an industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child can explain U.S. and Minnesota policies toward American Indian people in the late 1800s and 1900s and how these policies affected education, land, and citizenship for the Anishinaabe and Dakota.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the effects of reform movements on the political and social culture of Minnesota in the early twentieth century. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child can describe how reform movements changed Minnesota’s politics and society in the early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and impact of migration and immigration on Minnesota society during the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child can explain why people moved to Minnesota in the late 1800s and early 1900s and how immigration affected Minnesota’s society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the rise of big business, the growth of industry, the use of natural resources, and technological innovation influenced Minnesota's economy from 1860 to 1920. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child can explain how big business, industry growth, natural resource use, and new technology shaped Minnesota’s economy from 1860 to 1920.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain reasons for the United States-Dakota War of 1862; compare and contrast the perspectives of settlers and Dakota people before, during and after the war. (Civil War and Reconstruction: 1850-1877)
description_plain: Your child can explain why the United States-Dakota War of 1862 happened and compare how settlers and Dakota people saw the war before, during, and after it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create a timeline of the key events of the American Civil War; describe the war-time experiences of Minnesota soldiers and civilians. (Civil War and Reconstruction: 1850-1877)
description_plain: Your child can create a timeline of important Civil War events and describe what Minnesota soldiers and civilians experienced during the war.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes of the Civil War; describe how the debate over slavery and abolition played out in Minnesota. (Civil War and Reconstruction: 1850-1877)
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes of the Civil War and how debates about slavery and abolition affected Minnesota.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the process of how Minnesota became a territory and state; identify the key events, individuals and groups involved in the process. (Expansion and Reform: 1792-1861)
description_plain: Your child can describe how Minnesota became a territory and then a state, including important events and people involved.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how and why the United States and the Dakota and Anishinaabe negotiated treaties; describe the consequences of treaties on the Anishinaabe, Dakota and settlers in the upper Mississippi River region. (Expansion and Reform: 1792-1861)
description_plain: Your child can explain how and why the U.S. and Dakota and Anishinaabe people made treaties, and what effects these treaties had on the people and settlers in the upper Mississippi River area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how and why the United States claimed and settled the upper Mississippi River region in the early nineteenth century; explain the impact of steamboat transportation and settlement on the physical, social and cultural landscapes. (Expansion and Reform: 1792-1861)
description_plain: Your child can describe how and why the U.S. claimed and settled the upper Mississippi River region in the early 1800s and how steamboats and settlement changed the land and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe European exploration, competition and trade in the upper Mississippi River region; describe varied interactions between Minnesota's indigenous peoples and Europeans in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. (Colonization and Settlement: 1585-1763)
description_plain: Your child can describe European exploration, competition, and trade in the upper Mississippi River area and how Minnesota’s indigenous peoples interacted with Europeans in the 1600s and 1700s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the Dakota and Anishinaabe nations prior to 1800; describe their interactions with each other and other indigenous peoples. (Before European Contact)
description_plain: Your child can compare the Dakota and Anishinaabe nations before 1800 and describe how they interacted with each other and other indigenous groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Pose questions about a topic in Minnesota history, gather a variety of primary and secondary sources related to questions, analyze sources for credibility, identify possible answers, use evidence to draw conclusions, and present supported findings.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about Minnesota history, find and check different sources, think about possible answers, use evidence to decide, and share what they learned.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how land was used during different time periods in Minnesota history; explain how and why land use has changed over time.
description_plain: Your child can describe how land was used in Minnesota at different times and explain why land use has changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate, identify and describe major physical features in Minnesota; explain how physical features and the location of resources affect settlement patterns and the growth of cities in different parts of Minnesota.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe important physical features in Minnesota and explain how these features and resources affected where people settled and cities grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and use various kinds of maps, including overlaying thematic maps, of places in Minnesota; incorporate the "TODALSS" map basics, as well as points, lines and colored areas to display spatial information.
description_plain: Your child can make and use different kinds of maps of Minnesota, including adding layers and important map details to show information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why federal and state governments regulate economic activity to promote public well-being.
description_plain: Your child can explain why government rules help control the economy to keep people safe and healthy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the movement of goods and services, resources and money through markets in a market-based economy.
description_plain: Your child can describe how goods, services, resources, and money move through markets in an economy where people buy and sell.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe various types of income including wage, rent, interest and profit; explain the role that the development of human capital plays in determining one's income.
description_plain: Your child can name different ways people earn money, like wages, rent, interest, and profit, and understand how learning skills can help increase income.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create a budget based on a given monthly income, real-world expenses, and personal preferences, including enough savings to meet an identified future savings goal.
description_plain: Your child can make a budget using a monthly income, real expenses, and personal choices, including saving money for a future goal.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the concept of sovereignty and how treaty rights are exercised by the Anishinaabe and Dakota today.
description_plain: Your child understands what sovereignty means and how the Anishinaabe and Dakota use their treaty rights today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the basic structures, functions and ways of funding state and local governments.
description_plain: Your child can compare how state and local governments are organized, what they do, and how they get their money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the goals, offenses, penalties, long-term consequences, and privacy concerns of Minnesota's juvenile justice system.
description_plain: Your child can explain the goals, crimes, punishments, long-term effects, and privacy issues in Minnesota's juvenile justice system.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how laws are created; explain the differences between civil and criminal law; give examples of federal, state and local laws.
description_plain: Your child can describe how laws are made, know the difference between civil and criminal laws, and give examples of laws at the federal, state, and local levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major state and local (county, city, school board, township) governmental offices; describe the primary duties associated with them.
description_plain: Your child can name important state and local government offices and explain what each one does.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the purpose of Minnesota's Constitution; explain how the Minnesota Constitution organizes government and protects rights.
description_plain: Your child understands why Minnesota has a Constitution and how it organizes the government and protects people's rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define federalism and describe the relationship between the powers of the federal and state governments.
description_plain: Your child can explain federalism and how the federal and state governments share and divide their powers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relationship among the three branches of government: making laws by the legislative branch, implementing and enforcing laws by the executive branch, and interpreting laws by the judicial branch.
description_plain: Your child understands the three branches of government: the legislative branch makes laws, the executive branch carries out laws, and the judicial branch interprets laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define citizenship in the United States and explain that individuals become citizens by birth or naturalization
description_plain: Your child knows what it means to be a U.S. citizen and understands people become citizens by being born here or through naturalization.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the establishment and expansion of rights over time, including the impact of key court cases, state legislation and constitutional amendments.
description_plain: Your child can describe how rights have grown over time because of important court cases, laws, and changes to the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Address a state or local policy issue by identifying key opposing positions, determining conflicting values and beliefs, defending and justifying a position with evidence, and developing strategies to persuade others to adopt this position.
description_plain: Your child can look at a local or state issue, understand different opinions, explain their own view with reasons, and think of ways to convince others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use graphic data to analyze information about a public issue in state or local government.
description_plain: Your child can use charts and graphs to understand information about a public issue in their community or state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate arguments about selected issues from diverse perspectives and frames of reference, noting the strengths, weaknesses and consequences associated with the decision made on each issue.
description_plain: Your child can look at different sides of an issue, notice the good and bad points, and think about what happens because of the decisions made.

subject: Math
description: Identify pennies, nickels, dimes and quarters. Find the value of a group of coins and determine combinations of coins that equal a given amount.
description_plain: Your child can recognize pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters, add up groups of coins, and find different ways to make a certain amount.

subject: Math
description: Tell time to the quarter-hour and distinguish between a.m. and p.m.
description_plain: Your child can tell time to the nearest quarter-hour and understand the difference between a.m. and p.m.

subject: Math
description: Use time and money in real-world and mathematical situations.
description_plain: Your child can use money and time skills to solve everyday problems and math questions.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the relationship between length and the numbers on a ruler by using a ruler to measure lengths to the nearest centimeter or inch.
description_plain: Your child can use a ruler to measure how long things are in centimeters or inches.

subject: Math
description: Understand the relationship between the size of the unit of measurement and the number of units needed to measure the length of an object.
description_plain: Your child understands that smaller units mean you need more of them to measure the same length.

subject: Math
description: Understand length as a measurable attribute; use tools to measure length.
description_plain: Your child understands that length can be measured and uses tools like rulers to do it.

subject: Math
description: Identify and name basic two- and three-dimensional shapes, such as squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, trapezoids, hexagons, cubes, rectangular prisms, cones, cylinders and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can name and recognize basic flat shapes like squares and circles, and solid shapes like cubes and cones.

subject: Math
description: Describe, compare, and classify two- and three-dimensional figures according to number and shape of faces, and the number of sides, edges and vertices (corners).
description_plain: Your child can describe and sort shapes by how many sides, faces, edges, and corners they have.

subject: Math
description: Identify, describe and compare basic shapes according to their geometric attributes.
description_plain: Your child can identify, describe, and compare basic shapes by their features.

subject: Math
description: Use number sentences involving addition, subtraction, and unknowns to represent given problem situations. Use number sense and properties of addition and subtraction to find values for the unknowns that make the number sentences true.
description_plain: Your child can write number sentences with addition, subtraction, and missing numbers to show problem situations and find the missing values.

subject: Math
description: Understand how to interpret number sentences involving addition, subtraction and unknowns represented by letters. Use objects and number lines and create real-world situations to represent number sentences.
description_plain: Your child understands number sentences with addition, subtraction, and unknowns, and can use objects and number lines to explain them.

subject: Math
description: Use number sentences involving addition, subtraction and unknowns to represent and solve real-world and mathematical problems; create real-world situations corresponding to number sentences.
description_plain: Your child can use number sentences with addition, subtraction, and unknowns to solve problems and create real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Identify, create and describe simple number patterns involving repeated addition or subtraction, skip counting and arrays of objects such as counters or tiles. Use patterns to solve problems in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child can find and make simple number patterns using repeated adding or subtracting, skip counting, and groups of objects to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Recognize, create, describe, and use patterns and rules to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can recognize, create, describe, and use patterns and rules to solve everyday and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction to create and obtain information from tables, bar graphs and tally charts.
description_plain: Your child can use addition and subtraction to make and understand tables, bar graphs, and tally charts.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical addition and subtraction problems involving whole numbers with up to 2 digits.
description_plain: Your child can solve addition and subtraction problems with whole numbers up to two digits in real life and math.

subject: Math
description: Use mental strategies and algorithms based on knowledge of place value and equality to add and subtract two-digit numbers. Strategies may include decomposition, expanded notation, and partial sums and differences.
description_plain: Your child can use mental methods and written steps based on place value to add and subtract two-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: Estimate sums and differences up to 100.
description_plain: Your child can estimate sums and differences of numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with basic addition facts and related subtraction facts.
description_plain: Your child knows basic addition facts and the related subtraction facts quickly.

subject: Math
description: Use strategies to generate addition and subtraction facts including making tens, fact families, doubles plus or minus one, counting on, counting back, and the commutative and associative properties. Use the relationship between addition and subtraction to generate basic facts.
description_plain: Your child uses strategies like making tens, fact families, and counting on or back to learn addition and subtraction facts and understands how they relate.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate mastery of addition and subtraction basic facts; add and subtract one- and two-digit numbers in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child masters basic addition and subtraction facts and can add and subtract one- and two-digit numbers in real life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Compare and order whole numbers up to 1000.
description_plain: Your child can compare and put whole numbers up to 1000 in order.

subject: Math
description: Round numbers up to the nearest 10 and 100 and round numbers down to the nearest 10 and 100.
description_plain: Your child can round numbers up or down to the nearest 10 or 100.

subject: Math
description: Find 10 more or 10 less than a given three-digit number. Find 100 more or 100 less than a given three-digit number.
description_plain: Your child can find 10 more or less, and 100 more or less than a three-digit number.

subject: Math
description: Use place value to describe whole numbers between 10 and 1000 in terms of hundreds, tens and ones. Know that 100 is 10 tens, and 1000 is 10 hundreds.
description_plain: Your child can explain numbers between 10 and 1000 using hundreds, tens, and ones, and knows that 100 is 10 tens and 1000 is 10 hundreds.

subject: Math
