description: Read, write and represent whole numbers up to 1000. Representations may include numerals, addition, subtraction, multiplication, words, pictures, tally marks, number lines and manipulatives, such as bundles of sticks and base 10 blocks.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and show whole numbers up to 1000 using numbers, words, pictures, and objects like blocks or sticks.

subject: Math
description: Compare and represent whole numbers up to 1000 with an emphasis on place value and equality.
description_plain: Your child can compare whole numbers up to 1000 and understand their place value and when numbers are equal.

subject: Math
description: Collect, display and interpret data using frequency tables, bar graphs, picture graphs and number line plots having a variety of scales. Use appropriate titles, labels and units.
description_plain: Your child can collect information, make charts and graphs like bar graphs and picture graphs, and explain what the data shows using titles and labels.

subject: Math
description: Collect, organize, display, and interpret data. Use labels and a variety of scales and units in displays.
description_plain: Your child can gather, organize, and show data using different kinds of graphs and labels with correct units and scales.

subject: Math
description: Use an analog thermometer to determine temperature to the nearest degree in Fahrenheit and Celsius.
description_plain: Your child can read an analog thermometer to find the temperature in both Fahrenheit and Celsius to the nearest degree.

subject: Math
description: Make change up to one dollar in several different ways, including with as few coins as possible.
description_plain: Your child can figure out how to give change up to one dollar using different coins, including using the fewest coins possible.

subject: Math
description: Know relationships among units of time.
description_plain: Your child understands how different units of time relate to each other, like seconds, minutes, and hours.

subject: Math
description: Tell time to the minute, using digital and analog clocks. Determine elapsed time to the minute.
description_plain: Your child can tell time to the exact minute on both digital and analog clocks and figure out how much time has passed.

subject: Math
description: Use time, money and temperature to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use time, money, and temperature to solve everyday and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Measure distances around objects.
description_plain: Your child can measure the distance around objects.

subject: Math
description: Find the perimeter of a polygon by adding the lengths of the sides.
description_plain: Your child can find the perimeter of shapes by adding up the lengths of all their sides.

subject: Math
description: Use half units when measuring distances.
description_plain: Your child can use half units when measuring lengths.

subject: Math
description: Understand perimeter as a measurable attribute of real-world and mathematical objects. Use various tools to measure distances.
description_plain: Your child understands perimeter as the total distance around shapes and can measure it using different tools.

subject: Math
description: Sketch polygons with a given number of sides or vertices (corners), such as pentagons, hexagons and octagons.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes with a specific number of sides or corners, like pentagons, hexagons, and octagons.

subject: Math
description: Identify parallel and perpendicular lines in various contexts, and use them to describe and create geometric shapes, such as right triangles, rectangles, parallelograms and trapezoids.
description_plain: Your child can find and use parallel and perpendicular lines to describe and make shapes like rectangles and right triangles.

subject: Math
description: Use geometric attributes to describe and create shapes in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child can describe and create shapes by looking at their features like sides and angles.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplication and division basic facts to represent a given problem situation using a number sentence. Use number sense and multiplication and division basic facts to find values for the unknowns that make the number sentences true.
description_plain: Your child can write multiplication and division number sentences for problems and find missing numbers using basic facts and number sense.

subject: Math
description: Understand how to interpret number sentences involving multiplication and division basic facts and unknowns. Create real-world situations to represent number sentences.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication and division sentences with missing numbers and can make real-life problems that match those sentences.

subject: Math
description: Use number sentences involving multiplication and division basic facts and unknowns to represent and solve real-world and mathematical problems; create real-world situations corresponding to number sentences.
description_plain: Your child can use multiplication and division sentences with missing numbers to solve problems and create real-life examples for those sentences.

subject: Math
description: Create, describe, and apply single-operation input-output rules involving addition, subtraction and multiplication to solve problems in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child can create and use simple rules with addition, subtraction, or multiplication to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Use single-operation input-output rules to represent patterns and relationships and to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use simple math rules to find patterns and solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Order and compare unit fractions and fractions with like denominators by using models and an understanding of the concept of numerator and denominator.
description_plain: Your child can compare and order fractions with the same bottom number using pictures and understanding of numerators and denominators.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the size of a fractional part is relative to the size of the whole.
description_plain: Your child understands that the size of a fraction depends on the size of the whole thing it comes from.

subject: Math
description: Read and write fractions with words and symbols. Recognize that fractions can be used to represent parts of a whole, parts of a set, points on a number line, or distances on a number line.
description_plain: Your child can read and write fractions using words and symbols and knows fractions can show parts of a whole, parts of a group, or points on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand meanings and uses of fractions in real-world and mathematical situations.
description_plain: Your child understands what fractions mean and how to use them in real life and math.

subject: Math
description: Use strategies and algorithms based on knowledge of place value, equality and properties of addition and multiplication to multiply a two- or three-digit number by a one-digit number. Strategies may include mental strategies, partial products, the standard algorithm, and the commutative, associative, and distributive properties.
description_plain: Your child can multiply two- or three-digit numbers by one-digit numbers using different strategies like mental math, partial products, or standard methods.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving multiplication and division, including both "how many in each group" and "how many groups" division problems.
description_plain: Your child can solve multiplication and division problems from real life, including figuring out how many in each group or how many groups there are.

subject: Math
description: Represent multiplication facts by using a variety of approaches, such as repeated addition, equal-sized groups, arrays, area models, equal jumps on a number line and skip counting. Represent division facts by using a variety of approaches, such as repeated subtraction, equal sharing and forming equal groups. Recognize the relationship between multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child can show multiplication using repeated addition, groups, arrays, or number lines and show division using sharing or groups, understanding how multiplication and division relate.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction to solve real-world and mathematical problems involving whole numbers. Use various strategies, including the relationship between addition and subtraction, the use of technology, and the context of the problem to assess the reasonableness of results.
description_plain: Your child can use addition and subtraction to solve real-life and math problems and check if answers make sense using different strategies.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract multi-digit numbers, using efficient and generalizable procedures based on knowledge of place value, including standard algorithms.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract multi-digit numbers using place value and standard methods.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers; represent multiplication and division in various ways; solve real-world and mathematical problems using arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract large whole numbers, show multiplication and division in different ways, and solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Compare and order whole numbers up to 100,000.
description_plain: Your child can compare and put in order whole numbers up to 100,000.

subject: Math
description: Round numbers to the nearest 10,000, 1000, 100 and 10. Round up and round down to estimate sums and differences.
description_plain: Your child can round numbers to the nearest 10,000, 1,000, 100, or 10 and use rounding to estimate sums and differences.

subject: Math
description: Find 10,000 more or 10,000 less than a given five-digit number. Find 1000 more or 1000 less than a given four- or five-digit. Find 100 more or 100 less than a given four- or five-digit number.
description_plain: Your child can find numbers that are 10,000, 1,000, or 100 more or less than a given number with four or five digits.

subject: Math
description: Use place value to describe whole numbers between 1000 and 100,000 in terms of ten thousands, thousands, hundreds, tens and ones.
description_plain: Your child can explain whole numbers between 1,000 and 100,000 by breaking them into ten thousands, thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones.

subject: Math
description: Read, write and represent whole numbers up to 100,000. Representations may include numerals, expressions with operations, words, pictures, number lines, and manipulatives such as bundles of sticks and base 10 blocks.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and show whole numbers up to 100,000 using numbers, words, pictures, number lines, and objects like blocks.

subject: Math
description: Compare and represent whole numbers up to 100,000 with an emphasis on place value and equality.
description_plain: Your child can compare and show whole numbers up to 100,000, focusing on place value and when numbers are equal.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate on a map or globe the origins of peoples in the local community and state; create a timeline of when different groups arrived; describe why and how they came.
description_plain: Your child can find where different groups of people in your community and state came from on a map, make a timeline of when they arrived, and explain why and how they came.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps to compare and contrast a particular region in the United States, and also Canada or Mexico, at different points in time.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to compare a region in the U.S. with parts of Canada or Mexico at different times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of geographic factors on the development of modern agricultural regions in Minnesota and the United States.
description_plain: Your child can explain how geography affected farming areas in Minnesota and the U.S. today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the location of resources and the distribution of people and their various economic activities has created different regions in the United States and Canada.
description_plain: Your child can explain how where resources are found and where people live affects the different regions in the United States and Canada.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans adapt to and/or modify the physical environment and how they are in turn affected by these adaptations and modifications.
description_plain: Your child understands how people change or adjust to their environment and how these changes affect them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how geographic factors affect population distribution and the growth of cities in the United States and Canada.
description_plain: Your child can explain how land features influence where people live and how cities grow in the United States and Canada.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use data to analyze and explain the changing distribution of population in the United States and Canada over the last century.
description_plain: Your child uses data to show how the population in the United States and Canada has moved or changed over the last 100 years.

subject: Social Studies
description: Name and locate countries neighboring the United States and their major cities.
description_plain: Your child can name and find on a map the countries next to the United States and their main cities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Name and locate states and territories, major cities and state capitals in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can name and find on a map the states, territories, big cities, and capitals in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and identify the physical and human characteristics of places in the United States, and also Canada or Mexico.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe important natural and human-made features in places in the United States, Canada, or Mexico.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use photographs or satellite-produced images to interpret spatial information about the United States, and also Canada or Mexico.
description_plain: Your child can use photos or satellite images to understand information about places in the United States, Canada, or Mexico.

subject: Social Studies
description: Choose the most appropriate data from maps, charts, and graphs in an atlas to answer specific questions about geographic issues in the United States, and also Canada or Mexico.
description_plain: Your child can pick the best information from maps, charts, and graphs to answer questions about places in the United States, Canada, or Mexico.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use latitude and longitude on maps and globes to locate places in the United States, and also Canada or Mexico.
description_plain: Your child can use latitude and longitude on maps or globes to find places in the United States, Canada, or Mexico.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and use various kinds of maps, including overlaying thematic maps, of places in the United States, and also Canada or Mexico; incorporate the "TODALS" map basics, as well as points, lines and colored areas to display spatial information.
description_plain: Your child can make and use different kinds of maps showing places in the United States, Canada, or Mexico, including adding important map details and symbols.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe a market as any place or manner in which buyers and sellers interact to make exchanges; describe prices as payments of money for items exchanged in markets.
description_plain: Your child understands that a market is any place where buyers and sellers meet to trade, and that prices are the money paid for items.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define the productivity of a resource and describe ways to increase it.
description_plain: Your child can explain what makes a resource productive and ways to make it more productive.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply a reasoned decision-making process to make a choice.
description_plain: Your child can use careful thinking steps to make a choice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major roles and responsibilities of elected and appointed leaders in the community, state and nation; name some current leaders who function in these roles and how they are selected.
description_plain: Your child can name important jobs of leaders in the community, state, and nation, name some current leaders, and explain how they are chosen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe tribal government and some of the services it provides; distinguish between United States and tribal forms of government.
description_plain: Your child understands what tribal governments do and how they are different from the United States government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how people take action to influence a decision on a specific issue; explain how local, state, national or tribal governments have addressed that issue.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people try to influence decisions on issues and how different governments have handled those issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast daily life for people living in ancient times in at least three different regions of the world. (Post-Classical and Medieval Civilizations and Expanding Zones of Exchange: 600 CE – 1450 CE)
description_plain: Your child can compare daily life in ancient times in at least three different parts of the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify methods of communication used by peoples living in ancient times in three different regions of the world. (Classical Traditions, Belief Systems and Giant Empires: 2000 BCE - 600 CE)
description_plain: Your child can identify ways people communicated in ancient times in three different regions of the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the environment influenced the settlement of ancient peoples in three different regions of the world. (Early Civilizations and the Emergence of Pastoral Peoples: 8000 BCE-2000 BCE)
description_plain: Your child can explain how the environment affected where ancient people settled in three different parts of the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of individuals or groups who have had an impact on world history; explain how their actions helped shape the world around them.
description_plain: Your child can name people or groups who changed world history and explain how their actions made a difference.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how an invention of the past changed life at that time, including positive, negative and unintended outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how an invention from the past changed life, including good and bad effects.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast various ways that different cultures have expressed concepts of time and space.
description_plain: Your child can compare how different cultures think about time and space.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast two different accounts of an event.
description_plain: Your child can compare two different stories about the same event.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine historical records, maps and artifacts to answer basic questions about times and events in history, both ancient and more recent.
description_plain: Your child can use historical records, maps, and artifacts to answer questions about past events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create timelines of important events in three different time scales-decades, centuries and millennia
description_plain: Your child can make timelines showing important events using decades, centuries, and thousands of years.

subject: Social Studies
description: Reference different time periods using correct terminology, including the terms decade, century and millennium.
description_plain: Your child can use the words decade, century, and millennium correctly to talk about different time periods.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify physical and human features that act as boundaries or dividers; give examples of situations or reasons why people have made or used boundaries.
description_plain: Your child can identify natural and human-made boundaries and explain why people create or use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify landforms and patterns in population; explain why human populations are unevenly distributed around the world.
description_plain: Your child can name landforms and explain why people live unevenly in different parts of the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and interpret simple maps of places around the world, local to global; incorporate the "TODALS" map basics, as well as points, lines and colored areas to display spatial information.
description_plain: Your child can make and understand simple maps of places from local to global, including important map details and symbols.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps and concepts of location (relative location words and cardinal and intermediate directions) to describe places in one's community, the state of Minnesota, the United States or the world.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and location words like directions to describe places in their community, state, country, or the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that consumers have two roles-as sellers of resources and buyers of goods and services; explain that producers have two roles-as sellers of goods and services and buyers of resources.
description_plain: Your child understands that consumers both sell resources and buy goods, and producers both sell goods and buy resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that producing any good or service requires resources; describe the resources needed to produce a specific good or service; explain why it is not possible to produce an unlimited amount of a good or service.
description_plain: Your child knows that making goods or services needs resources and can explain why we can't make unlimited amounts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe income as the money earned from selling resources and expenditures as the money used to buy goods and services.
description_plain: Your child understands that income is money earned from selling resources and expenditures are money spent to buy things.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify possible short- and long-term consequences (costs and benefits) of different choices.
description_plain: Your child can identify possible short- and long-term good and bad results of different choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the three branches of government (executive, legislative, and judicial) and their primary functions.
description_plain: Your child can name the three branches of government and explain what each one does.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the importance of the services provided by government; explain that they are funded through taxes and fees.
description_plain: Your child understands why government services are important and that taxes and fees pay for them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of civic discourse (including speaking, listening, voting and respecting diverse viewpoints) and the principles of majority rule and minority rights.
description_plain: Your child can explain why talking, listening, voting, and respecting others' views are important in government, and understands majority rule and minority rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways people make a difference in the civic life of their communities, state, nation or world by working as individuals or groups to address a specific problem or need.
description_plain: Your child can name ways people help their communities or countries by working alone or with others to solve problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the changing relations between the United States and other countries around the world in the beginning of the twenty-first century. (The United States in a New Global Age: 1980-present)
description_plain: Your child can explain how the United States' relationships with other countries have changed since 1980.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how new technologies have changed political, economic and social interactions. (The United States in a New Global Age: 1980-present)
description_plain: Your child can explain how new technologies have changed how people interact in politics, the economy, and society since 1980.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the goals and tactics of the Civil Rights Movement, the American Indian Movement, and the Women's Rights Movement; explain the advantages and disadvantages of non-violent resistance. (Post-World War II United States: 1945-1989)
description_plain: Your child can compare the goals and methods of the Civil Rights, American Indian, and Women's Rights movements and explain the pros and cons of peaceful protests.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the changing role of the federal government in reshaping post-war society. (Post-World War II United States: 1945-1989)
description_plain: Your child understands how the federal government changed its role to help rebuild society after World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the economic boom and social transformation experienced by postwar United States. (Post-World War II United States: 1945-1989)
description_plain: Your child can describe the economic growth and social changes that happened in the U.S. after World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the involvement and role of the United States in global conflicts and acts of cooperation. (Post-World War II United States: 1945-1989)
description_plain: Your child can compare how the U.S. took part in wars and worked with other countries after World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the social and political effects of the Cold War on the people of the United States. (Post-World War II United States: 1945-1989)
description_plain: Your child understands how the Cold War affected the daily lives and politics of people in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify military and non-military actions taken by the United States during the Cold War to resist the spread of communism. (Post-World War II United States: 1945-1989)
description_plain: Your child can name military and non-military actions the U.S. used during the Cold War to stop communism from spreading.

subject: Social Studies
description: Outline the causes and conduct of World War II including the nations involved, major political and military figures and key battles, and the Holocaust. (The Great Depression and World War II: 1920-1945)
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes, major events, important leaders, key battles, and the Holocaust during World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Outline how the United States mobilized its economic and military resources during World War II; describe the impact of the war on domestic affairs. (The Great Depression and World War II: 1920-1945)
description_plain: Your child understands how the U.S. used its economy and military during World War II and how the war affected life at home.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the impact of the Great Depression on United States society, including ethnic and racial minorities, and how government responded to events with New Deal policies. (The Great Depression and World War II: 1920-1945)
description_plain: Your child can describe how the Great Depression affected different groups in the U.S. and how the government responded with New Deal programs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify causes of the Great Depression and factors that led to an extended period of economic collapse in the United States. (The Great Depression and World War II: 1920-1945)
description_plain: Your child can identify what caused the Great Depression and why the economy stayed weak for a long time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the political impact of World War I, including the formation of the League of Nations and renewed United States isolationism until World War II. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child understands how World War I affected politics, including the creation of the League of Nations and the U.S. staying out of world affairs until World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Outline the causes and conduct of World War I including the nations involved, major political and military figures, and key battles. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes, countries involved, important leaders, and key battles of World War I.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the changing role of the United States regarding its neighboring regions and its expanding sphere of influence around the world. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child understands how the U.S. changed its role with nearby countries and grew its influence worldwide during this time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the strategies used by suffragists in their campaigns to secure the right to vote; identify the Nineteenth Amendment. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child can describe how women fought for the right to vote and knows about the Nineteenth Amendment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effects of racism and legalized segregation on American society, including the compromise of 1876, the rise of "Jim Crow," immigration restriction, and the relocation of American Indian tribes to reservations. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child understands how racism and segregation affected American society, including laws and policies that hurt minorities and Native Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast reform movements at the turn of the twentieth century. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child can compare different reform movements around 1900 and explain how they were alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the consequences of economic transformation on migration, immigration, politics and public policy at the turn of the twentieth century. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child understands how big economic changes affected where people lived, immigration, politics, and government policies around 1900.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the impact of the United States Industrial Revolution on the production, consumption and distribution of goods. (Development of an Industrial United States: 1870-1920)
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Industrial Revolution changed how goods were made, bought, and shared in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the effects of the Civil War on Americans in the north, south and west, including liberated African-Americans, women, former slaveholders and indigenous peoples. (Civil War and Reconstruction: 1850-1877)
description_plain: Your child can describe how the Civil War affected people in the North, South, and West, including freed African Americans, women, former slave owners, and Native Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Outline the major political and military events of the Civil War; evaluate how economics and foreign and domestic politics affected the outcome of the war. (Civil War and Reconstruction: 1850-1877)
description_plain: Your child understands the major events of the Civil War and how economics and politics influenced its outcome.

subject: Social Studies
description: Cite the main ideas of the debate over slavery and states' rights; explain how they resulted in major political compromises and, ultimately, war. (Civil War and Reconstruction: 1850-1877)
description_plain: Your child can explain the main ideas behind the debate over slavery and states' rights and how these led to political compromises and eventually war.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify causes and consequences of Antebellum reform movements including abolition and women's rights. (Expansion and Reform: 1792-1861)
description_plain: Your child can identify the causes and effects of reform movements before the Civil War, like ending slavery and fighting for women's rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify new technologies and innovations that transformed the United States' economy and society; explain how they influenced political and regional development. (Expansion and Reform: 1792-1861)
description_plain: Your child understands how new inventions changed the U.S. economy and society and affected politics and regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the processes that led to the territorial expansion of the United States, including the Louisiana Purchase and other land purchases, wars and treaties with foreign and indigenous nations, and annexation.(Expansion and Reform: 1792-1861)
description_plain: Your child can describe how the U.S. grew bigger by buying land, fighting wars, making treaties, and adding new territories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the distribution and political status of indigenous populations in the United States and Canada; describe how their status has evolved throughout the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.
description_plain: Your child can compare where Indigenous peoples lived and their political status in the U.S. and Canada and explain how this changed in the 1800s and 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Pose questions about a topic in United States history, gather and organize a variety of primary and secondary sources related to the questions, analyze sources for credibility and bias; suggest possible answers and write a thesis statement; use sources to draw conclusions and support the thesis; present supported findings, and cite sources.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about U.S. history, find and organize different sources, check if they are trustworthy, form answers, write a main idea, use sources to support it, and share their findings with proper credit.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and use various kinds of maps, including overlaying thematic maps, of places in the United States; incorporate the "TODALSS" map basics, as well as points, lines and colored areas to display spatial information.
description_plain: Your child can make and use different types of maps of U.S. places, including special maps that show themes, using map basics like titles, orientation, and colors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe profit as an incentive for an individual to take the risks associated with creating and producing new goods or starting a business in an existing market; give examples of how the pursuit of profit can lead to undesirable, as well as desirable, effects.
description_plain: Your child understands that people try to make money by creating or selling goods and knows that trying to earn profit can have both good and bad results.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the interaction of buyers (through demand) and sellers (through supply) determines price in a market.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the prices of things are decided by how much buyers want them and how much sellers offer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how items are allocated or rationed when scarcity exists.
description_plain: Your child understands how things are shared or limited when there is not enough for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply reasoned decision-making techniques in making choices; explain why different households or groups faced with the same alternatives might make different choices.
description_plain: Your child can make thoughtful choices and explain why different families or groups might choose differently when faced with the same options.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe diplomacy and other foreign policy tools; cite historical cases in which the United States government used these tools.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the U.S. uses diplomacy and other tools to handle relationships with other countries and give examples from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how changes in election processes over time contributed to freer and fairer elections.
description_plain: Your child understands how changes in voting rules over time have made elections more fair and open.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the amendment process and the impact of key constitutional amendments.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the Constitution is changed through amendments and explain the effects of important amendments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the Constitution and the Bill of Rights limits the government and the governed, protects individual rights, supports the principle of majority rule while protecting the rights of the minority, and promotes the general welfare.
description_plain: Your child understands how the Constitution and Bill of Rights limit government power, protect individual rights, support majority rule, protect minorities, and promote the common good.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe historical applications of the principle of checks and balances within the United States government.
description_plain: Your child can describe examples from history showing how the U.S. government uses checks and balances to share power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the rights and responsibilities of citizens, non-citizens and dual citizens.
description_plain: Your child can compare the rights and duties of citizens, non-citizens, and people with dual citizenship.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the components of responsible citizenship including informed voting and decision making, developing and defending positions on public policy issues, and monitoring and influencing public decision making.
description_plain: Your child understands what responsible citizenship means, including voting wisely, sharing opinions on public issues, and staying involved in government decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain landmark Supreme Court decisions involving the Bill of Rights and other individual protections; explain how these decisions helped define the scope and limits of personal, political and economic rights.
description_plain: Your child can explain important Supreme Court cases about the Bill of Rights and individual protections and how these cases define personal and political rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of how principles expressed in the Declaration of Independence and Preamble to the Constitution have been applied throughout United States history, including how they have evolved (if applicable) over time.
description_plain: Your child can find examples of how ideas from the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution have been used and changed throughout U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Exhibit civic skills including participating in civic discussion on issues in the contemporary United States, demonstrating respect for the opinions of people or groups who have different perspectives, and reaching consensus.
description_plain: Your child can join conversations about current U.S. issues, listen respectfully to different opinions, and work with others to agree on solutions.

subject: Math
