description: Describe how the culture of a community reflects the history, daily life or beliefs of its people.
description_plain: Your child can describe how a community’s culture shows its history, daily life, and beliefs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast daily life for Minnesota Dakota or Anishinaabe peoples in different times, including before European contact and today.
description_plain: Your child can compare daily life of Minnesota Dakota or Anishinaabe people long ago and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use historical records and artifacts to describe how people's lives have changed over time.
description_plain: Your child can use old records and objects to explain how people’s lives have changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use and create calendars to identify days, weeks, months, years and seasons; use and create timelines to chronicle personal, school, community or world events.
description_plain: Your child can use and make calendars and timelines to show days, weeks, months, years, seasons, and important events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify causes and consequences of human impact on the environment and ways that the environment influences people
description_plain: Your child can name causes and effects of how people change the environment and how the environment affects people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps, photos, or other geographic tools to answer basic questions about where people are located.c
description_plain: Your child can use maps and photos to answer simple questions about where people live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps, photos or other geographic tools to identify and locate major landmarks or major physical features of the United States
description_plain: Your child can use maps and photos to find important landmarks and natural features in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate key features on a map or globe; use cardinal directions to describe the relationship between two or more features.
description_plain: Your child can find key places on a map or globe and use directions like north, south, east, and west to explain where they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create sketch maps to illustrate detailed spatial information about settings from stories; describe the spatial information found on the maps.
description_plain: Your child can draw simple maps from stories and explain what the maps show about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify money as any generally accepted item used in making exchanges.
description_plain: Your child understands that money is anything people accept to buy and sell things.

subject: Social Studies
description: Classify materials that come from nature as natural resources (or raw materials); tools, equipment and factories as capital resources; and workers as human resources.
description_plain: Your child can sort things from nature as natural resources, tools and factories as capital resources, and people who work as human resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the trade-offs of a decision; describe the opportunity cost of a choice as the next best alternative which was not chosen.
description_plain: Your child can explain that choosing one thing means giving up another and describe what was missed by making that choice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Given a goal and several alternative choices to reach that goal, select the best choice and explain why.
description_plain: Your child can pick the best choice to reach a goal and explain why it is the best.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast student rules, rights and responsibilities at school with their rules, rights and responsibilities at home; explain the importance of obeying rules.
description_plain: Your child can compare rules, rights, and responsibilities at school and at home and explain why following rules is important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of constitutions.
description_plain: Your child understands why constitutions are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate voting skills, identify rules that keep a voting process fair, and explain why voting is important.
description_plain: Your child can show how to vote fairly, know the rules that keep voting fair, and explain why voting matters.

subject: Math
description: Calculate experimental probabilities from experiments; represent them as percents, fractions and decimals between 0 and 1 inclusive. Use experimental probabilities to make predictions when actual probabilities are unknown.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to find chances of events happening and show these chances as percents, fractions, or decimals to make predictions.

subject: Math
description: Perform experiments for situations in which the probabilities are known, compare the resulting relative frequencies with the known probabilities; know that there may be differences.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments when chances are known and compare the results to the expected chances, knowing they might be different.

subject: Math
description: Determine the probability of an event using the ratio between the size of the event and the size of the sample space; represent probabilities as percents, fractions and decimals between 0 and 1 inclusive. Understand that probabilities measure likelihood.
description_plain: Your child can find the chance of an event by comparing the number of ways it can happen to all possible outcomes and show this as a percent, fraction, or decimal.

subject: Math
description: Determine the sample space (set of possible outcomes) for a given experiment and determine which members of the sample space are related to certain events. Sample space may be determined by the use of tree diagrams, tables or pictorial representations.
description_plain: Your child can list all possible outcomes of an experiment using tools like tree diagrams or tables and find which outcomes belong to certain events.

subject: Math
description: Use probabilities to solve real-world and mathematical problems; represent probabilities using fractions, decimals and percents.
description_plain: Your child can use chances to solve real problems and show these chances as fractions, decimals, or percents.

subject: Math
description: Estimate weights, capacities and geometric measurements using benchmarks in measurement systems with appropriate units.
description_plain: Your child can estimate weights, volumes, and measurements using common reference points and correct units.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems in various contexts involving conversion of weights, capacities, geometric measurements and times within measurement systems using appropriate units.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems by converting weights, volumes, measurements, and time using the right units.

subject: Math
description: Choose appropriate units of measurement and use ratios to convert within measurement systems to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can pick the right units and use ratios to convert measurements to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Develop and use formulas for the sums of the interior angles of polygons by decomposing them into triangles.
description_plain: Your child can find formulas for the total inside angles of shapes by breaking them into triangles.

subject: Math
description: Determine missing angle measures in a triangle using the fact that the sum of the interior angles of a triangle is 180°. Use models of triangles to illustrate this fact.
description_plain: Your child can find missing angles in triangles knowing all inside angles add up to 180 degrees and use models to show this.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems using the relationships between the angles formed by intersecting lines.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using the relationships between angles made by crossing lines.

subject: Math
description: Understand and use relationships between angles in geometric figures.
description_plain: Your child understands and uses angle relationships in shapes.

subject: Math
description: Estimate the perimeter and area of irregular figures on a grid when they cannot be decomposed into common figures and use correct units, such as cm and cm².
description_plain: Your child can estimate the perimeter and area of odd-shaped figures on a grid and use the right units like centimeters and square centimeters.

subject: Math
description: Calculate the area of quadrilaterals. Quadrilaterals include squares, rectangles, rhombuses, parallelograms, trapezoids and kites. When formulas are used, be able to explain why they are valid.
description_plain: Your child can calculate the area of different four-sided shapes and explain why the formulas work.

subject: Math
description: Calculate the surface area and volume of prisms and use appropriate units, such as cm² and cm³. Justify the formulas used. Justification may involve decomposition, nets or other models.
description_plain: Your child can find the surface area and volume of prism shapes using units like square centimeters and cubic centimeters, and explain how the formulas work using pictures or models.

subject: Math
description: Calculate perimeter, area, surface area and volume of two- and three-dimensional figures to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can calculate perimeter, area, surface area, and volume of flat and solid shapes to solve everyday and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve equations involving positive rational numbers using number sense, properties of arithmetic and the idea of maintaining equality on both sides of the equation. Interpret a solution in the original context and assess the reasonableness of results.
description_plain: Your child can solve equations with positive fractions or decimals by keeping both sides balanced and check if the answers make sense in the problem.

subject: Math
description: Represent real-world or mathematical situations using equations and inequalities involving variables and positive rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write equations and inequalities with variables and positive numbers to show real-life or math situations.

subject: Math
description: Understand and interpret equations and inequalities involving variables and positive rational numbers. Use equations and inequalities to represent realworld and mathematical problems; use the idea of maintaining equality to solve equations. Interpret solutions in the original context.
description_plain: Your child understands equations and inequalities with variables and positive numbers, uses them to represent problems, solves equations by keeping both sides equal, and explains the answers in context.

subject: Math
description: Apply the associative, commutative and distributive properties and order of operations to generate equivalent expressions and to solve problems involving positive rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules like grouping, order, and distributing to rewrite expressions and solve problems with positive numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use properties of arithmetic to generate equivalent numerical expressions and evaluate expressions involving positive rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use math properties to create equal expressions and solve expressions with positive numbers.

subject: Math
description: Represent the relationship between two varying quantities with function rules, graphs and tables; translate between any two of these representations.
description_plain: Your child can show how two changing amounts relate using rules, graphs, and tables, and switch between these forms.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a variable can be used to represent a quantity that can change, often in relationship to another changing quantity. Use variables in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands that a variable stands for a number that can change and uses variables in different situations.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and represent relationships between varying quantities; translate from one representation to another; use patterns, tables, graphs and rules to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and show how changing amounts relate using patterns, tables, graphs, and rules to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Estimate solutions to problems with whole numbers, fractions and decimals and use the estimates to assess the reasonableness of results in the context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can make good guesses for answers with whole numbers, fractions, and decimals, and use those guesses to check if answers seem right.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems requiring arithmetic with decimals, fractions and mixed numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with decimals, fractions, and mixed numbers.

subject: Math
description: Calculate the percent of a number and determine what percent one number is of another number to solve problems in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child can find the percent of a number and figure out what percent one number is of another to solve different problems.

subject: Math
description: Use the meanings of fractions, multiplication, division and the inverse relationship between multiplication and division to make sense of procedures for multiplying and dividing fractions.
description_plain: Your child understands how multiplying and dividing fractions work by thinking about what fractions mean and how multiplication and division undo each other.

subject: Math
description: Multiply and divide decimals and fractions, using efficient and generalizable procedures, including standard algorithms.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide decimals and fractions using easy and standard methods.

subject: Math
description: Multiply and divide decimals, fractions and mixed numbers; solve real-world and mathematical problems using arithmetic with positive rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide decimals, fractions, and mixed numbers and use these skills to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use reasoning about multiplication and division to solve ratio and rate problems.
description_plain: Your child can use multiplication and division ideas to solve problems about ratios and rates.

subject: Math
description: Determine the rate for ratios of quantities with different units.
description_plain: Your child can find the rate when comparing two amounts with different units.

subject: Math
description: Apply the relationship between ratios, equivalent fractions and percents to solve problems in various contexts, including those involving mixtures and concentrations.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios, fractions, and percents to solve problems, including those about mixtures and concentrations.

subject: Math
description: Identify and use ratios to compare quantities; understand that comparing quantities using ratios is not the same as comparing quantities using subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios to compare amounts and knows that comparing with ratios is different from subtracting.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of ratio and its relationship to fractions and to the multiplication and division of whole numbers. Use ratios to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child understands what ratios are, how they relate to fractions and multiplication/division, and uses ratios to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Convert between equivalent representations of positive rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can change positive rational numbers into different but equal forms.

subject: Math
description: Determine greatest common factors and least common multiples. Use common factors and common multiples to calculate with fractions and find equivalent fractions.
description_plain: Your child can find the biggest common factors and smallest common multiples to help with fraction calculations and find equal fractions.

subject: Math
description: Factor whole numbers; express a whole number as a product of prime factors with exponents.
description_plain: Your child can break down whole numbers into prime factors and write them using exponents.

subject: Math
description: Determine equivalences among fractions, decimals and percents; select among these representations to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can find equal values among fractions, decimals, and percents and choose the best form to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand that percent represents parts out of 100 and ratios to 100.
description_plain: Your child understands that percent means parts out of 100 or ratios to 100.

subject: Math
description: Compare positive rational numbers represented in various forms. Use the symbols < , = and >.
description_plain: Your child can compare positive rational numbers in different forms using <, =, and > symbols.

subject: Math
description: Locate positive rational numbers on a number line and plot pairs of positive rational numbers on a coordinate grid.
description_plain: Your child can find positive rational numbers on a number line and plot pairs of these numbers on a grid.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, represent and compare positive rational numbers expressed as fractions, decimals, percents and ratios; write positive integers as products of factors; use these representations in real-world and mathematical situations.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, show, and compare positive rational numbers as fractions, decimals, percents, and ratios, and use these in real life and math.

subject: Math
description: Use proportional reasoning to draw conclusions about and predict relative frequencies of outcomes based on probabilities.
description_plain: Your child can use proportional thinking to predict how often things will happen based on probability.

subject: Math
description: Calculate probability as a fraction of sample space or as a fraction of area. Express probabilities as percents, decimals and fractions.
description_plain: Your child can find probability as a fraction of all possible outcomes or area, and express it as a percent, decimal, or fraction.

subject: Math
description: Use random numbers generated by a calculator or a spreadsheet or taken from a table to simulate situations involving randomness, make a histogram to display the results, and compare the results to known probabilities.
description_plain: Your child can use random numbers from a calculator or table to simulate chance events, make a bar graph of results, and compare these to expected probabilities.

subject: Math
description: Calculate probabilities and reason about probabilities using proportions to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can calculate and think about probabilities using proportions to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use reasoning with proportions to display and interpret data in circle graphs (pie charts) and histograms. Choose the appropriate data display and know how to create the display using a spreadsheet or other graphing technology.
description_plain: Your child can use proportions to create and understand circle graphs (pie charts) and bar graphs, and knows how to make these using a computer or calculator.

subject: Math
description: Display and interpret data in a variety of ways, including circle graphs and histograms.
description_plain: Your child can show and understand data in different ways, including pie charts and bar graphs.

subject: Math
description: Describe the impact that inserting or deleting a data point has on the mean and the median of a data set. Know how to create data displays using a spreadsheet to examine this impact.
description_plain: Your child can explain how adding or removing a data point changes the average and middle value of a data set, and use a computer to see these changes.

subject: Math
description: Design simple experiments and collect data. Determine mean, median and range for quantitative data and from data represented in a display. Use these quantities to draw conclusions about the data, compare different data sets, and make predictions.
description_plain: Your child can plan simple experiments, collect data, find the average, middle, and range values, and use these to understand and compare data and make predictions.

subject: Math
description: Use mean, median and range to draw conclusions about data and make predictions.
description_plain: Your child can use average, middle, and range to understand data and make predictions.

subject: Math
description: Graph and describe translations and reflections of figures on a coordinate grid and determine the coordinates of the vertices of the figure after the transformation.
description_plain: Your child can move and flip shapes on a grid, describe these changes, and find the new corner points.

subject: Math
description: Use proportions and ratios to solve problems involving scale drawings and conversions of measurement units.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios and proportions to solve problems with scale drawings and changing measurement units.

subject: Math
description: Apply scale factors, length ratios and area ratios to determine side lengths and areas of similar geometric figures.
description_plain: Your child can use scale factors and ratios to find side lengths and areas of shapes that look the same but are different sizes.

subject: Math
description: Describe the properties of similarity, compare geometric figures for similarity, and determine scale factors.
description_plain: Your child can explain what makes shapes similar, compare shapes to see if they are similar, and find the scale factor between them.

subject: Math
description: Analyze the effect of change of scale, translations and reflections on the attributes of two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child understands how changing the size, moving, or flipping flat shapes affects their properties.

subject: Math
description: Calculate the volume and surface area of cylinders and justify the formulas used.
description_plain: Your child can calculate the volume and surface area of cylinders and explain why the formulas work.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the proportional relationship between the diameter and circumference of a circle and that the unit rate (constant of proportionality) is p . Calculate the circumference and area of circles and sectors of circles to solve problems in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands how the diameter and circumference of a circle are related by pi, and can find the circumference and area of circles and parts of circles to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Use reasoning with proportions and ratios to determine measurements, justify formulas and solve real-world and mathematical problems involving circles and related geometric figures.
description_plain: Your child uses ratios and proportions to find measurements, explain formulas, and solve problems involving circles and similar shapes.

subject: Math
description: Solve equations resulting from proportional relationships in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child can solve equations that come from proportional relationships in different situations.

subject: Math
description: Represent relationships in various contexts with equations involving variables and positive and negative rational numbers. Use the properties of equality to solve for the value of a variable. Interpret the solution in the original context.
description_plain: Your child can write equations with variables to show relationships, solve for unknowns using equality rules, and explain what the answers mean.

subject: Math
description: Represent real-world and mathematical situations using equations with variables. Solve equations symbolically, using the properties of equality. Also solve equations graphically and numerically. Interpret solutions in the original context.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve equations with variables to represent real-life and math problems, using different methods and explain the answers.

subject: Math
description: Apply understanding of order of operations and grouping symbols when using calculators and other technologies.
description_plain: Your child knows how to use the correct order of operations and grouping symbols when using calculators or technology.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate algebraic expressions containing rational numbers and whole number exponents at specified values of their variables.
description_plain: Your child can find the value of algebra expressions with rational numbers and whole number exponents by plugging in given numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use properties of algebra to generate equivalent numerical and algebraic expressions containing rational numbers, grouping symbols and whole number exponents. Properties of algebra include associative, commutative and distributive laws.
description_plain: Your child can use algebra rules to rewrite expressions with rational numbers, grouping symbols, and exponents in different but equal ways.

subject: Math
description: Apply understanding of order of operations and algebraic properties to generate equivalent numerical and algebraic expressions containing positive and negative rational numbers and grouping symbols; evaluate such expressions.
description_plain: Your child applies order of operations and algebra rules to create and evaluate equivalent expressions with positive and negative rational numbers and grouping symbols.

subject: Math
description: Represent real-world or mathematical situations using equations and inequalities involving variables and positive and negative rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write equations and inequalities with variables and positive or negative rational numbers to represent real-life or math situations.

subject: Math
description: Use knowledge of proportions to assess the reasonableness of solutions.
description_plain: Your child uses what they know about proportions to check if answers to problems make sense.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step problems involving proportional relationships in numerous contexts.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems that involve proportional relationships in many different situations.

subject: Math
description: Represent proportional relationships with tables, verbal descriptions, symbols, equations and graphs; translate from one representation to another. Determine the unit rate (constant of proportionality or slope) given any of these representations.
description_plain: Your child can show proportional relationships using tables, words, symbols, equations, and graphs, and find the unit rate from any of these.

subject: Math
description: Recognize proportional relationships in real-world and mathematical situations; represent these and other relationships with tables, verbal descriptions, symbols and graphs; solve problems involving proportional relationships and explain results in the original context.
description_plain: Your child recognizes proportional relationships in real life and math, represents them in different ways, solves related problems, and explains the answers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the graph of a proportional relationship is a line through the origin whose slope is the unit rate (constant of proportionality). Know how to use graphing technology to examine what happens to a line when the unit rate is changed.
description_plain: Your child understands that a proportional relationship graphs as a line through zero with a slope equal to the unit rate, and can use graphing tools to see how changing the unit rate changes the line.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a relationship between two variables, x and y, is proportional if it can be expressed in the form y/x=k or y =kx . Distinguish proportional relationships from other relationships, including inversely proportional relationships ( xy =k or y=k/x ).
description_plain: Your child understands that two variables are proportional if their ratio is constant, and can tell proportional relationships apart from other types like inverse proportionality.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of proportionality in real-world and mathematical situations, and distinguish between proportional and other relationships.
description_plain: Your child understands what proportionality means in real life and math, and can tell proportional relationships apart from other kinds.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the relationship between the absolute value of a rational number and distance on a number line. Use the symbol for absolute value.
description_plain: Your child understands that the absolute value of a number shows its distance from zero on a number line and can use the absolute value symbol.

subject: Math
description: Use proportional reasoning to solve problems involving ratios in various contexts.
description_plain: Your child uses proportional reasoning to solve problems involving ratios in different situations.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems in various contexts involving calculations with positive and negative rational numbers and positive integer exponents, including computing simple and compound interest.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using positive and negative rational numbers and whole number exponents, including calculating simple and compound interest.

subject: Math
description: Understand that calculators and other computing technologies often truncate or round numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands that calculators and other tools often round or cut off numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use real-world contexts and the inverse relationship between addition and subtraction to explain why the procedures of arithmetic with negative rational numbers make sense.
description_plain: Your child uses real-life examples and the opposite relationship between addition and subtraction to understand why math rules for negative numbers work.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, multiply and divide positive and negative rational numbers that are integers, fractions and terminating decimals; use efficient and generalizable procedures, including standard algorithms; raise positive rational numbers to whole-number exponents.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide positive and negative rational numbers, including fractions and decimals, and can raise positive rational numbers to whole number powers using efficient methods.

subject: Math
description: Calculate with positive and negative rational numbers, and rational numbers with whole number exponents, to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can calculate with positive and negative rational numbers and whole number exponents to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and generate equivalent representations of positive and negative rational numbers, including equivalent fractions.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and create different but equal forms of positive and negative rational numbers, like equivalent fractions.

subject: Math
description: Compare positive and negative rational numbers expressed in various forms using the symbols < , > , = , ≤ , ≥.
description_plain: Your child can compare positive and negative rational numbers in different forms using symbols like <, >, =, ≤, and ≥.

subject: Math
description: Locate positive and negative rational numbers on a number line, understand the concept of opposites, and plot pairs of positive and negative rational numbers on a coordinate grid.
description_plain: Your child can find positive and negative rational numbers on a number line, understand opposites, and plot pairs of these numbers on a coordinate grid.

subject: Math
description: Understand that division of two integers will always result in a rational number. Use this information to interpret the decimal result of a division problem when using a calculator.
description_plain: Your child understands that dividing two integers always gives a rational number and can interpret decimal answers from calculators.

subject: Math
description: Know that every rational number can be written as the ratio of two integers or as a terminating or repeating decimal. Recognize that p is not rational, but that it can be approximated by rational numbers such as 22/7 and 3.14.
description_plain: Your child knows that every rational number can be written as a fraction or a decimal that ends or repeats, and understands that pi is not rational but can be approximated by numbers like 22/7 or 3.14.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, represent and compare positive and negative rational numbers, expressed as integers, fractions and decimals.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, show, and compare positive and negative rational numbers as whole numbers, fractions, and decimals.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the major issues that were debated at the Constitutional Convention. (Revolution and a New Nation: 1754-1800)
description_plain: Your child can explain the main issues people debated during the Constitutional Convention.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the successes and failures of the national government under the Articles of Confederation and why it was ultimately discarded and replaced with the Constitution. (Revolution and a New Nation: 1754-1800)
description_plain: Your child can describe what worked and didn’t work under the Articles of Confederation and why it was replaced by the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the purposes of the founding documents and explain the basic principles of democracy that were set forth in the Declaration of Independence, the Constitution and the Bill of Rights. (Revolution and a New Nation: 1754-1800)
description_plain: Your child can explain the purposes of the Declaration of Independence, Constitution, and Bill of Rights and the basic ideas of democracy they include.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the impact of the American Revolution on different groups within the 13 colonies that made up the new United States. (Revolution and a New Nation: 1754-1800)
description_plain: Your child can compare how the American Revolution affected different groups living in the 13 colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major events of the American Revolution culminating in the creation of a new and independent nation. (Revolution and a New Nation: 1754-1800)
description_plain: Your child can identify the major events of the American Revolution that led to the United States becoming independent.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the development of self-governance in the British colonies and explain the influence of this tradition on the American Revolution. (Revolution and a New Nation: 1754-1800)
description_plain: Your child can describe how the British colonies started governing themselves and how this influenced the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major conflicts between the colonies and England following the Seven Years War; explain how these conflicts led to the American Revolution. (Revolution and a New Nation: 1754-1800)
description_plain: Your child can name the main problems between the colonies and England after the Seven Years War and explain how these problems led to the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe ways that enslaved people and people in free black communities resisted slavery and transferred, developed and maintained their cultural identities. (Colonization and Settlement: 1585-1763)
description_plain: Your child understands how enslaved people and free Black communities fought against slavery and kept their cultures alive.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast life within the English, French and Spanish colonies in North America. (Colonization and Settlement: 1585-1763)
description_plain: Your child can compare what life was like in English, French, and Spanish colonies in North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the role of Europeans and West Africans in the development of the Atlantic slave trade. (Colonization and Settlement: 1585-1763)
description_plain: Your child knows how Europeans and West Africans were involved in starting and growing the Atlantic slave trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe early interactions between indigenous peoples, Europeans and Africans, including the Columbian Exchange; identify the consequences of those interactions on the three groups. (Colonization and Settlement: 1585-1763)
description_plain: Your child can describe how Native peoples, Europeans, and Africans first interacted, including the exchange of goods and ideas, and what happened because of these meetings.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify various motivations of Europeans for exploration and settlement in Asia, Africa and the Americas from the fifteenth to early seventeenth centuries. (Colonization and Settlement: 1585-1763)
description_plain: Your child understands why Europeans explored and settled in Asia, Africa, and the Americas from the 1400s to early 1600s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe complex urban societies that existed in Mesoamerica and North America before 1500. (Before European Contact)
description_plain: Your child can describe the complex cities and societies that existed in Mesoamerica and North America before Europeans arrived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple causes and outcomes of a historical event.
description_plain: Your child can explain several causes and effects of a historical event.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain a historical event from multiple perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can explain a historical event by looking at it from different people's points of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: Pose questions about a topic in history, examine a variety of sources related to the questions, interpret findings and use evidence to draw conclusions that address the questions.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about history, look at different sources to find answers, and use evidence to explain what they learned.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the construct of an era; interpret the connections between three or more events in an era depicted on a timeline or flowchart.
description_plain: Your child understands what a time period (era) is and can explain how three or more events in that time are connected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how geographic factors affected land use in the North American colonies.
description_plain: Your child can explain how geography affected how land was used in the North American colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and identify the physical and human characteristics of places in the North American colonies.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe physical features and human-made features of places in the North American colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and use various kinds of maps, including overlaying thematic maps, of places in the North American colonies; incorporate the "TODALS" map basics, as well as points, lines and colored areas to display spatial information.
description_plain: Your child can make and use different kinds of maps about the North American colonies, including adding important map details and showing information with points, lines, and colors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the concept of profit as the motivation for entrepreneurs; calculate profit as the difference between revenue (from selling goods and services) and cost (payments for resources used).
description_plain: Your child understands that making a profit motivates business owners and can figure out profit by subtracting costs from money earned.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe various uses of income and discuss advantages and disadvantages of each.
description_plain: Your child can talk about different ways people use money and the good and bad sides of each way.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply a decision-making process to identify an alternative choice that could have been made for a historical event; explain the probable impact of that choice.
description_plain: Your child can think about a different choice that could have been made in history and explain what might have happened because of that choice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how law limits the powers of government and the governed, protects individual rights and promotes the general welfare.
description_plain: Your child understands how laws limit government power, protect people's rights, and help the community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify taxes and fees collected, and services provided, by governments during colonial times; compare these to the taxes and fees collected, and services provided, by the government today.
description_plain: Your child can name taxes and fees collected and services provided by governments during colonial times and compare them to those today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how governmental power is limited through the principles of federalism, the separation of powers, and checks and balances.
description_plain: Your child understands how government power is limited by sharing power between federal and state governments, separating powers, and checking each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the primary functions of the three branches of government and how the leaders of each branch are selected, as established in the United States Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can explain the main jobs of the three branches of government and how leaders are chosen, as described in the U.S. Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain specific protections that the Bill of Rights provides to individuals and the importance of these ten amendments to the ratification of the United States Constitution.
description_plain: Your child understands the rights protected by the Bill of Rights and why these ten amendments were important to approving the U.S. Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historically significant people during the period of the American Revolution; explain how their actions contributed to the development of American political culture.
description_plain: Your child can name important people from the American Revolution and explain how their actions helped shape American political ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify a public problem in the school or community, analyze the issue from multiple perspectives, and create an action plan to address it.
description_plain: Your child can find a problem in their school or community, look at it from different views, and make a plan to fix it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Simulate a historic event to show how civic engagement (voting, civil discourse about controversial issues and civic action) improves and sustains a democratic society, supports the general welfare, and protects the rights of individuals.
description_plain: Your child can act out a historic event to show how voting, talking about issues, and taking action help keep a democracy strong and protect people's rights.

subject: Science
description: Apply place-based evidence, including those from Minnesota American Indian Tribes and communities and other cultures, to construct an explanation of how a warming climate impacts the hydrosphere, geosphere, biosphere, or atmosphere. (P: 8, CC: 4, CI: ESS3) Examples of cultures may include those within the local context of the learning community and within the context of Minnesota . Emphasis is on understanding and using American Indian knowledge systems to describe regional impacts of climate change to Minnesota. Examples may include the water cycle and how precipitation change over time impacts cultural practices related to nibi (“water” in the Ojibwe language); or the decline/species loss of wiigwaas (“paper birch” in the Ojibwe language and an important tree in Anishinaabe culture) due to climate stressors like drought or changes in snow cover.
description_plain: Your child can use local and Native American knowledge to explain how climate change affects water, land, plants, or air in Minnesota.

subject: Science
description: Apply place-based evidence, including those from Minnesota American Indian Tribes and communities and other cultures, to construct an explanation of how a warming climate impacts the hydrosphere, geosphere, biosphere, or atmosphere. (P: 8, CC: 4, CI: ESS3) Examples of cultures may include those within the local context of the learning community and within the context of Minnesota . Emphasis is on understanding and using American Indian knowledge systems to describe regional impacts of climate change to Minnesota. Examples may include the water cycle and how precipitation change over time impacts cultural practices related to nibi (“water” in the Ojibwe language); or the decline/species loss of wiigwaas (“paper birch” in the Ojibwe language and an important tree in Anishinaabe culture) due to climate stressors like drought or changes in snow cover.
description_plain: Your child can use local and Native American knowledge to explain how climate change affects water, land, plants, or air in Minnesota.

subject: Science
description: Apply place-based evidence, including those from Minnesota American Indian Tribes and communities and other cultures, to construct an explanation of how a warming climate impacts the hydrosphere, geosphere, biosphere, or atmosphere. (P: 8, CC: 4, CI: ESS3) Examples of cultures may include those within the local context of the learning community and within the context of Minnesota . Emphasis is on understanding and using American Indian knowledge systems to describe regional impacts of climate change to Minnesota. Examples may include the water cycle and how precipitation change over time impacts cultural practices related to nibi (“water” in the Ojibwe language); or the decline/species loss of wiigwaas (“paper birch” in the Ojibwe language and an important tree in Anishinaabe culture) due to climate stressors like drought or changes in snow cover.
description_plain: Your child can use local and Native American knowledge to explain how climate change affects water, land, plants, or air in Minnesota.

subject: Science
description: Apply place-based evidence, including those from Minnesota American Indian Tribes and communities and other cultures, to construct an explanation of how a warming climate impacts the hydrosphere, geosphere, biosphere, or atmosphere. (P: 8, CC: 4, CI: ESS3) Examples of cultures may include those within the local context of the learning community and within the context of Minnesota . Emphasis is on understanding and using American Indian knowledge systems to describe regional impacts of climate change to Minnesota. Examples may include the water cycle and how precipitation change over time impacts cultural practices related to nibi (“water” in the Ojibwe language); or the decline/species loss of wiigwaas (“paper birch” in the Ojibwe language and an important tree in Anishinaabe culture) due to climate stressors like drought or changes in snow cover.
description_plain: Your child can use local and Native American knowledge to explain how climate change affects water, land, plants, or air in Minnesota.

subject: Science
description: Compare, integrate and evaluate sources of information in order to determine how specific factors, including human activity, impact the groundwater system of a region. (P: 8, CC: 2, CI: ESS2, ETS2) Emphasis is on the making sense of technical information presented in a variety of formats (graphs, diagrams and words). Example of sources of information may include student experimental data. Examples of factors may include porosity, permeability, sediment or rock type, recharge or discharge factors, and potential energy. Examples of human factors may include usage rates, run-off, agricultural practices, and loss of wetlands.
description_plain: Your child can compare and understand different information to see how things like human actions affect groundwater in an area.

subject: Science
description: Compare, integrate and evaluate sources of information in order to determine how specific factors, including human activity, impact the groundwater system of a region. (P: 8, CC: 2, CI: ESS2, ETS2) Emphasis is on the making sense of technical information presented in a variety of formats (graphs, diagrams and words). Example of sources of information may include student experimental data. Examples of factors may include porosity, permeability, sediment or rock type, recharge or discharge factors, and potential energy. Examples of human factors may include usage rates, run-off, agricultural practices, and loss of wetlands.
description_plain: Your child can compare and understand different information to see how things like human actions affect groundwater in an area.

subject: Science
description: Compare, integrate and evaluate sources of information in order to determine how specific factors, including human activity, impact the groundwater system of a region. (P: 8, CC: 2, CI: ESS2, ETS2) Emphasis is on the making sense of technical information presented in a variety of formats (graphs, diagrams and words). Example of sources of information may include student experimental data. Examples of factors may include porosity, permeability, sediment or rock type, recharge or discharge factors, and potential energy. Examples of human factors may include usage rates, run-off, agricultural practices, and loss of wetlands.
description_plain: Your child can compare and understand different information to see how things like human actions affect groundwater in an area.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate competing design solutions for developing, managing, and utilizing energy and mineral resources based on cost-benefit ratios.* (P: 7, CC: 5, CI: ESS3, ETS1) Emphasis is on the conservation, recycling, and reuse of resources (such as minerals, metals or soils) where possible, and on minimizing impacts where it is not. Examples include developing best practices for agricultural soil use, mining (for fracking sand, iron ore, and rare metals), and pumping (for oil and natural gas).
description_plain: Your child can compare different ways to use energy and minerals and decide which is best by looking at costs and benefits, including ways to save and reuse resources.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate the evidence and reasoning for the explanatory model that Earth's interior is layered and that thermal convection drives the cycling of matter. (P: 7, CC: 5, CI: ESS2) Emphasis is on how plate tectonics is controlled by mantle convection (due to the outward flow of energy from the decay of radioactive isotopes and the gravitational movement of denser materials toward the interior).
description_plain: Your child can explain the evidence that Earth's inside has layers and that heat movement causes materials to cycle inside the Earth.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate the evidence of the past and current movements of continental and oceanic crust and the theory of plate tectonics to explain the ages of crustal rocks. (P: 7, CC: 1, CI: ESS1) Emphasis is on evaluating the strengths, weaknesses and reliability of the given evidence along with its ability to support logical and reasonable arguments about the motion and age of crustal plates. Examples of evidence may include the ages of oceanic crust which increase with distance from mid-ocean ridges (a result of seafloor spreading), the ages of North American continental crust decreasing with distance away from a central ancient core (a result of past plate interactions).
description_plain: Your child can look at evidence about how Earth's crust has moved over time and use it to explain the ages of rocks on land and under the ocean.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate or refine a technological solution to reduce the human impacts on a natural system and base the evaluations or refinements on evidence and analysis of pertinent data.* (P: 6, CC: 7, CI: ESS3, ETS1, ETS2) Emphasis is on prioritizing identified criteria and constraints related to social and environmental considerations. Examples of data for the impacts of human activities may include the quantities and types of pollutants released into air or groundwater, changes to biomass and species diversity, or areal changes in land surface use (for surface mining, urban development, or agriculture). Examples for limiting impacts may range from local efforts (such as reducing, reusing, and recycling resources) to large-scale geoengineering design solutions (such as altering global temperatures by making large changes to the atmosphere or ocean).
description_plain: Your child can improve or change technology that reduces human harm to nature by using evidence and data to make decisions.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific reasoning and evidence from ancient Earth materials, meteorites, and other planetary surfaces to construct an account of Earth’s formation and early history. (P:6, CC: 7, CI: ESS1) Emphasis of the practice is on linking the evidence to the claims about Earth’s formation. Emphasis of the core idea is on using available evidence within the solar system to reconstruct the early history of Earth. Examples of evidence include the absolute ages of ancient materials, the sizes and compositions of solar system objects, and the impact cratering record of planetary surfaces.
description_plain: Your child can use scientific evidence from old Earth materials and space objects to explain how Earth formed and its early history.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation that links astronomical evidence of light spectra, motion of distant galaxies, and composition of matter in the universe to the Big Bang. (P: 6, CC: 5, CI: ESS1, ETS2) Emphasis is on how the redshift of light from galaxies is an indication of cosmic expansion, on how the cosmic microwave background radiation is a remnant of the Big Bang, and on how the observed composition of ordinary matter, primarily found in stars and interstellar gases, matches that predicted by the Big Bang.
description_plain: Your child can explain the Big Bang by using evidence like light from galaxies, movement of galaxies, and what the universe is made of.

subject: Science
description: Use a model to describe how variations in the flow of energy into and out of Earth’s systems result in changes in climate. (P: 2, CC: 2, CI: ESS2). Emphasis is on using a model to describe the mechanism for how energy flow affects changes in climate. Examples of the causes of climate change differ by timescale and may include: 1 - 10 years: large volcanic eruptions, ocean circulation; 10-100s of years: changes in human activity, ocean circulation, solar output; 10 - 100s of thousands of years: changes to Earth's orbit and the orientation of its axis; and 10 - 100s of millions of years: long term changes in atmospheric composition.
description_plain: Your child can use a model to explain how changes in energy flowing into and out of Earth's systems cause climate to change over different time periods.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe how unequal heating and rotation of the Earth cause patterns of atmospheric and oceanic circulation that determine regional climates. (P: 2, CC: 4, CI: ESS2) Emphasis is on how patterns vary by latitude, altitude, and geographic land distribution. Emphasis of atmospheric circulation is on the sunlight-driven latitudinal banding, the Coriolis effect, and resulting prevailing winds; emphasis of ocean circulation is on the transfer of heat by the global ocean currents, which is constrained by the Coriolis effect and the outlines of continents. Examples of models may be diagrams, maps and globes, or digital representations.
description_plain: Your child can create and use a model to show how uneven heating and Earth's rotation create wind and ocean current patterns that affect regional climates.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model based on evidence to explain how Earth’s internal and surface processes operate at different spatial and temporal scales to form continental and ocean-floor features. (P: 2, CC: 7, CI: ESS2) Emphasis is on how the appearance of land features (such as mountains, and valleys), and seafloor features (such as trenches and ridges) are a result of both constructive mechanisms (such as volcanism, and tectonic motion) and destructive mechanisms (such as weathering, and coastal erosion). Examples specific to Minnesota may include features formed relatively recently during continental glaciation and volcanic features that have long since been eroded away.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to explain how Earth's internal and surface processes shape landforms like mountains and ocean features like trenches over time.

description: Develop and use a model based on evidence to explain how Earth’s internal and surface processes operate at different spatial and temporal scales to form continental and ocean-floor features. (P: 2, CC: 7, CI: ESS2) Emphasis is on how the appearance of land features (such as mountains, and valleys), and seafloor features (such as trenches and ridges) are a result of both constructive mechanisms (such as volcanism, and tectonic motion) and destructive mechanisms (such as weathering, and coastal erosion). Examples specific to Minnesota may include features formed relatively recently during continental glaciation and volcanic features that have long since been eroded away.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to explain how Earth's internal and surface processes shape landforms like mountains and ocean features like trenches over time.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model based on evidence to illustrate the life span of the sun and the role of nuclear fusion in the sun’s core to release energy that eventually reaches Earth in the form of radiation. (P: 2, CC: 3, CI: ESS1) Emphasis is on showing the relationships among the fuel, products and the energy transfer mechanisms that allow energy from nuclear fusion in the sun's core to reach the Earth. Examples of evidence that students might use include the masses and life times of other stars, as well as the ways that the sun's radiation varies due to sudden solar flares, sunspot cycles, and non-cyclic variations over the centuries.
description_plain: Your child can use a model to show how the sun produces energy through nuclear fusion and how that energy travels to Earth as radiation.

subject: Science
description: Develop or use an algorithmic representation, based on investigations of causes and effects in complex Earth systems, to illustrate the relationships within some part of the Earth system and how human activity might affect those relationships. (P: 5, CC: 4, CI: ESS3, ETS2) Emphasis is on students identifying the interacting components of a system, mathematically modeling how those factors interact and accounting for the effects of human activity on the system. Examples may include local systems in which natural and human-influenced variables impact the amount of runoff.
description_plain: Your child can develop a step-by-step model to show how parts of Earth's systems interact and how human actions can change those interactions.

subject: Science
description: Develop a computational model, based on observational data, experimental evidence, and chemical theory, to describe the cycling of carbon among Earth’s systems.** (P: 2, CC: 5, CI: ESS2) Emphasis is on quantitative modeling of carbon as it cycles through the ocean, air, rock (particularly limestone), soil, and organisms. Emphasis is also on using empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to inform the algorithmic thinking about the conservation and cycling of matter.
description_plain: Your child can create a computer model using data and science to explain how carbon moves through the ocean, air, soil, rocks, and living things.

description: Use mathematical and computational representations to predict the motion of natural and human-made objects that are in orbit in the solar system.** (P: 5, CC: 3, CI: ESS1, ETS2) Emphasis is on Kepler’s laws of planetary motion and Newtonian gravitational laws governing orbital motions, which apply to human-made satellites as well as planets and moons.
description_plain: Your child can use math and computer models to predict how natural and human-made objects move in space, like planets and satellites.

subject: Science
description: Analyze geoscience data and the results from global climate models to make an evidence-based forecast of the current rate of global or regional climate change and associated future impacts to Earth's systems and human infrastructure.* (P: 4, CC: 7, ESS3, ETS1) Examples of evidence (for both data and climate model outputs) may include precipitation and temperature and their associated impacts on sea level, glacial ice volumes, and atmosphere and ocean composition. Engineering examples may include using climate change data (rising sea levels) to evaluate the impact on the ability of sewer system to handle runoff or of existing wells to produce potable water.
description_plain: Your child can study climate data and models to predict how fast climate is changing and what effects this will have on Earth's systems and human structures.

subject: Science
description: Analyze geoscience data to make a claim that one change to the Earth's surface can create feedbacks that cause changes to other Earth systems. (P: 4, CC: 7, CI: ESS2, ETS2) Emphasis is on using data analysis tools and techniques in order to make valid scientific claims. Examples may include climate feedback mechanisms, such as how an increase in greenhouse gases causes a rise in global temperatures that melt glaciers and sea ice, which reduces the amount of sunlight reflected from the Earth's surface (albedo), increasing surface temperatures and further reducing the amount of ice. Examples may also be taken from other system interactions, such as how the loss of ground vegetation causes an increase in water runoff and soil erosion; how dammed rivers increase groundwater recharge, decrease sediment transport, and increase coastal erosion; or how the loss of wetlands causes a decrease in local humidity that further reduces the wetland extent and longevity.
description_plain: Your child can analyze data to explain how one change on Earth's surface can cause other changes in Earth's systems through feedback loops.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation of the properties of soils to model the effects of human activity on soil resources. (P: 3, CC: 2, CI: ESS3, ETS2) Emphasis is on identifying variables to test, developing a workable experimental design, and identifying limitations of the data. Examples of variables may include soil type and composition (particularly those found in Minnesota), erosion rate, water infiltration rates, nutrient profiles, soil conservation practices, or specific crop requirements.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to study soil properties and how human activities affect soil.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation of the properties of water and its effects on Earth materials and surface processes. (P: 3, CC: 6, CI: ESS2) Emphasis is on physical and chemical investigations with water and a variety of solid materials to provide the evidence for how processes in the water cycle and rock cycle interact. Examples of physical investigations may include transportation and deposition of various sediment types and sizes, erosion of surfaces with varying amounts of soil moisture content and/or ground cover, or frost wedging by the expansion of water as it freezes. Examples of chemical investigations may include chemical weathering and recrystallization (by testing the solubility of different materials) or melt generation (by examining how water lowers the melting temperature of most solids). Examples specific to Minnesota may include chemical weathering of limestone to create karst topography.
description_plain: Your child can plan and carry out an investigation to study water’s properties and how it affects Earth materials and surface processes.

description: Obtain and communicate information about how Minnesota American Indian Tribes and communities and other cultures construct solutions to mitigate threats to biodiversity.* (P: 8, CC: 7, CI: LS2, ETS1) Examples of cultures may include those within the local context of the learning community and within the context of Minnesota . description: Communicate scientific information that common ancestry and biological evolution are supported by multiple lines of empirical evidence. (P: 8, CC: 1, CI: LS4) Emphasis is on conceptual understanding of the role each line of evidence has relating to common ancestry and biological evolution. Examples of evidence may include similarities in DNA sequences, the fossil record, artificial selection, anatomical structures, and the order of appearance of structures in embryological development.

description: Evaluate evidence for the role of group behavior on an individual’s and species’ chances to survive and reproduce. (P: 7, CC: 2, CI: LS2) Emphasis of the practice is on identifying evidence supporting the outcomes of group behavior, and developing logical and reasonable arguments based on evidence. Emphasis of the core idea is on distinguishing between group and individual behavior. Examples of group behavior may include herding, migratory behaviors, or various symbioses .

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how natural selection leads to the adaptation of populations. (P: 6, CC: 2, CI: LS4) Emphasis is on using data to provide evidence for how specific biotic and abiotic differences in ecosystems contribute to a change in gene frequency over time, leading to adaptation of populations. Examples of selective forces may include long-term climate change, or variations in seasonal temperatures, pH, light cycles, geographic barriers, or in response to the evolution of other organisms.
description_plain: Your child can explain how natural selection causes populations to adapt by using evidence about how environmental differences change gene frequencies over time.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation based on evidence that the process of evolution primarily results from four factors: reproduction within a species, heritable genetic variation of individuals in that species, competition for limited resources, and increased survival and reproduction of the individuals best suited for the environment. (P: 6, CC: 2, CI: LS4) Emphasis is on using evidence to explain the influence each of the four factors has on the number, behavior, morphology, or physiology of organisms, in terms of their ability to compete for limited resources and subsequent survival of individuals and adaptation of their species. Examples of evidence may include mathematical models such as simple distribution graphs and proportional reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can explain using evidence that evolution happens because of reproduction, genetic differences, competition for resources, and survival of the best-suited individuals.

(P: 2, CC: 4, CI: LS1) Emphasis is on illustrating inputs and outputs of matter and the transfer and transformation of energy in photosynthesis by plants and other photosynthesizing organisms. Examples of models may include diagrams, chemical equations, and conceptual models.
description_plain: Your child can use a model to show how plants change light into stored energy through photosynthesis, including what goes in and what comes out.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to illustrate the levels of organization of interacting systems and how that translates into specific functions in multicellular organisms. (P: 2, CC: 6, CI: LS1) Emphasis is on specific functions at the organ system level such as nutrient uptake, water delivery, and organism movement in response to neural stimuli. Examples of models may include real (e.g. fish, birds, insects, etc.) or imaginary organisms with attention to the various structures and systems that assist the organism in performing necessary life functions.
description_plain: Your child can create and use a model to show how parts of living things work together to do jobs like taking in food, moving water, and reacting to signals.

subject: Science
description: Use a computational model to support claims for the cycling of matter and flow of energy among organisms in an ecosystem.** (P: 5, CC: 5, CI: LS2) Examples of claims about matter cycles may include how carbon, nitrogen, or water cycles through the environment, and/or how disruptions to those systems affect matter cycling. Examples of energy flow may include the transfer of the sun’s energy into and among organisms, and/or connections between fossil fuel burning and the carbon cycle. Examples of computational models may include online simulations and animated representations.
description_plain: Your child can use computer models to show how matter and energy move through living things in an ecosystem.

subject: Science
description: Use a computational model to support or revise an evidence-based explanation for factors that have ecological and economic impacts on different sized ecosystems, including factors caused by the practices of various human groups.** (P: 5, CC: 3, CI: LS2) Examples of ecological impacts might include changes in the carrying capacity, species numbers and/or types of organisms present in an environment. Examples of human practices that can have positive or negative impacts, such as stream restoration versus deforestation as an ecological example. Examples of computational models may include online simulations of population dynamics, population ecology, or population growth.
description_plain: Your child can use computer models to explain how human activities affect ecosystems of different sizes.

description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability to support explanations that organisms with an advantageous heritable trait tend to increase in proportion to organisms lacking this trait. (P: 4, CC: 1, CI: LS4) Emphasis is on analyzing shifts in the numerical distribution of traits and using these shifts as evidence to support explanations. Examples of advantageous traits may antibiotic resistance in bacteria, or the coloration and camouflage of animals in response to changing environmental conditions.
description_plain: Your child can use statistics and probability to explain how traits that help organisms survive become more common.

subject: Science
description: Communicate and evaluate claims by various stakeholders, including Minnesota American Indian Tribes and communities and other cultures, about the environmental impacts of various chemical processes on natural resources. (P: 8, CC: 2, CI: PS1) Examples of cultures may include those within the local context of the learning community and within the context of Minnesota. Examples of natural resources may include wild rice harvesting, mining of minerals, and access to clean air and water. Examples of chemical processes may include sulfate in water/soil, acid mine drainage, and air and water pollution.
description_plain: Your child can share and evaluate different views, including those of Minnesota American Indian Tribes, about how chemical processes affect natural resources like water and air.

subject: Science
description: Review text and online sources to develop a series of questions regarding the chemistry, utility, and safety of nuclear fission. (P: 8, CC: 7,CI: PS1) Emphasis is on evaluating the argument and specific claims in the text including the validity of reasoning as well as the relevance and sufficiency of the evidence. Examples may include fission (nuclear power generation, nuclear weapons) and the use of fission byproducts (nuclear medicine, food irradiation).
description_plain: Your child can read and ask questions about the chemistry, uses, and safety of nuclear fission, like in power plants or medicine.

Examples may include fission (nuclear power generation, nuclear weapons) and the use of fission byproducts (nuclear medicine, food irradiation).
description_plain: Your child can read and ask questions about the chemistry, uses, and safety of nuclear fission, like in power plants or medicine.

description: Communicate scientific and technical information about why the molecular-level structure is important in the functioning of designed materials.* (P: 8, CC: 6, CI: PS1) Emphasis is on the attractive and repulsive forces that determine the functioning of the material. description_plain: Your child can explain why the tiny structure of materials matters for how they work, like why metals conduct electricity.

description: Construct an explanation for the phenomenon of solution creation and identify from patterns how the properties of the resulting solution depend on the interactions between solute and solvent or on concentrations of solutes. (P: 6, CC: 1, CI: PS1) Emphasis is on polarity, solubility, boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, and osmosis. Examples may include salts dissolving to make water hard, road salt, antifreeze, oil spills, reverse osmosis water systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how solutions form and how their properties depend on how substances mix and their amounts.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific principles and evidence to provide an explanation about the effects of changing the surface area, agitation, temperature, and concentration of the reacting particles on the rate at which the reaction occurs. (P: 6, CC: 1, CI: PS1) Emphasis is on student reasoning that focuses on the number and energy of collisions between molecules and on simple reactions in which there are only two reactants. Examples of evidence may include temperature, concentration, and rate data; and qualitative relationships between rate and temperature.
description_plain: Your child can explain how changing things like temperature or stirring affects how fast chemical reactions happen.

description: Develop models to illustrate the changes in the composition of the nucleus of the atom and the energy released during the processes of fission, fusion, and radioactive decay. (P: 2, CC: 5, CI: PS1) Emphasis is on simple qualitative models and on the scale of energy released in nuclear processes relative to other kinds of transformations. Not included is quantitative calculations of the energy released.
description_plain: Your child can create models to show how the nucleus of atoms changes and releases energy during fission, fusion, and radioactive decay.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model based on evidence to illustrate that the release or absorption of energy from a chemical reaction system depends upon the changes in total bond energy. (P: 2, CC: 5, CI: PS1) Emphasis of the practice is on illustrating the relationships between components of the system. Emphasis of the core idea is on how a chemical reaction is a system that affects the energy change. Examples of models may include molecular level drawings, diagrams, graphs showing the relative energies of reactants and products, and representations showing energy is conserved. Not included is the calculation of the total bond energy change during a chemical reaction from the bond energies of reactants and products.
description_plain: Your child can make models to show how energy is released or absorbed during chemical reactions based on bond changes.

subject: Science
description: Use mathematical representations to support the claim that atoms, and therefore mass, are conserved during a chemical reaction. (P: 5, CC: 5, CI: PS1) Emphasis of the practice is on using mathematical ideas to communicate the proportional relationships between the masses of atoms in the reactants and products. Emphasis of the core idea is on the translation of these relationships to the macroscopic scale using the mole as the conversion from the atomic to the macroscopic scale.
description_plain: Your child can use math to show that atoms and mass stay the same during chemical reactions by comparing the weights of atoms before and after.

description: Develop a data simulation, based on observations and experimental data of how the pressure, volume temperature, and mass of a gas are related to each other, to predict the effect on a system of changing one of those variables.** (P: 5, CC: 2, CI: PS1) Emphasis is on applying the kinetic molecular theory of gases to develop gas laws. Example systems may include balloons, tires, or syringes.
description_plain: Your child can create a data-based model to predict how changing pressure, volume, temperature, or mass affects gases like in balloons or tires.

subject: Science
description: Analyze patterns in air or water quality data to make claims about the causes and severity of a problem and the necessity to remediate or to recommend a treatment process. (P: 4, CC :2, CI: PS3) Emphasis is on the scale of the problem and appropriate use of concentration units. Examples of pollutant data may include ozone, lead, particulates, nitrates, or microorganisms. Examples of remediation may include physical, chemical or biological processes.
description_plain: Your child can study air or water quality data to explain pollution problems and suggest ways to fix them.

description: Plan and conduct an investigation of acid-base reactions to test ideas about the concentrations of the hydronium ion in an aqueous solution (pH). (P:3, CC: 3, CI: PS1) Emphasis is on developing an understanding of pH scales and various ways to measure pH. Also included is understanding the relative strength of acidity based on periodic properties of elements, the electronegativity model of electron distribution, empirical dipole moments, and molecular geometry. Examples of investigations may include household chemicals and ocean acidification analogs.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do experiments with acids and bases to learn about pH and how acidity is measured.
